title.zh: 卷五十六 張王種陳列傳
title.en: Volume 56: Biographies of Zhang, Wang, Zhong, Chen
title.raw: 卷五十六 張王種陳列傳 Volume 56: Biographies of Zhang, Wang, Zhong, Chen

sentences.zh: 張□字叔明，犍為武陽人也。

translations.literal: Zhang [lacuna in text], styled Shuming, was a native of Wuyang in Qianwei commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Hao (the patronymic graph is missing in some manuscripts), styled Shuming, came from Wuyang in Qianwei.

sentences.zh: 六世祖良，高帝時為太子少傅，封留侯。

translations.literal: His sixth-generation ancestor Liang, in the time of the High Emperor, served as junior tutor to the heir apparent and was enfeoffed as Marquis of Liu.
translations.idiomatic: Six generations back, Zhang Liang had been junior tutor to the crown prince under Gaozu and held the title Marquis of Liu.

sentences.zh: □少遊學京師，*

translations.literal: Zhang [lacuna in text] in his youth traveled to study in the capital,*
translations.idiomatic: In his youth Zhang Hao studied in Luoyang; the sentence breaks in the received text.

translations.literal: (At first)
translations.idiomatic: At first,

sentences.zh: *永元中，歸仕州郡，辟大將軍鄧騭府，五遷尚書僕射，職事八年，出為彭城相。

translations.literal: *In the Yongyuan era he returned to serve in the commanderies, was summoned to Grand General Deng Zhi's office, was promoted five times to vice director of the masters of writing, held office eight years, then went out as chancellor of Pengcheng.
translations.idiomatic: During Yongyuan he took posts in the provinces, entered Grand General Deng Zhi's headquarters, rose through five steps to vice director of the masters of writing, served eight years, and was then sent out as chancellor of Pengcheng.

translations.literal: [One]
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: (footnote marker)

sentences.zh: 注[一]明帝子彭城王恭之相也。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: He was chancellor to Prince Gong of Pengcheng, a son of Emperor Ming.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: That is, he served Liu Gong, Mingdi's son who was Prince of Pengcheng.

sentences.zh: 永寧元年，征拜廷尉。

translations.literal: In the first year of Yongning he was summoned and appointed minister of justice.
translations.idiomatic: In 120 CE he was recalled to the capital as minister of justice.

sentences.zh: □雖非法家，而留心刑斷，數與尚書辯正疑獄，多以詳當見從。

translations.literal: Zhang [lacuna in text], though not a specialist in legalist doctrine, gave close attention to criminal judgments; he repeatedly joined the masters of writing in debating doubtful cases, and his thorough, balanced opinions were mostly followed.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Hao was no narrow legalist, yet he weighed penal cases with care and, debating doubtful suits with the masters of writing, usually carried the day with well-reasoned views.

sentences.zh: [一]時安帝廢皇太子為濟陰王，□與太常桓焉、太僕來歷廷爭之，不能得。

translations.literal: [One] At that time Emperor An had deposed the imperial heir as Prince of Jiyin; [Zhang] together with Grand Master of Ceremonies Huan Yan and Grand Coachman Lai Li argued the matter at court but could not prevail.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: When An deposed the crown prince to the rank of Prince of Jiyin, Zhang Hao joined Huan Yan and Lai Li in protesting at court, without success.

sentences.zh: 事已具來歷傳。

translations.literal: The affair is fully set out in Lai Li's biography.
translations.idiomatic: The episode is narrated in full in the biography of Lai Li.

sentences.zh: 退而上疏曰：「昔賊臣江充，造構讒逆，至令戾園興兵，終及禍難。

translations.literal: He withdrew and submitted a memorial saying: 'In former times the traitorous minister Jiang Chong fabricated slander and rebellion until the Li Garden raised arms and disaster followed in the end.'
translations.idiomatic: He then memorialized: 'Long ago Jiang Chong forged charges that drove the Li heir to revolt and brought catastrophe in train.'

sentences.zh: [二]後壺關三老一言，上乃覺悟，雖追前失，悔之何逮！

translations.literal: [Two] After a single remonstrance from the Sangu elder of Huguan the emperor awoke to the truth; though he could pursue past errors, what good is regret once it is too late!'
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: One honest plea from the Huguan elder opened Wu's eyes—too late for vain regret.'

sentences.zh: [三]今皇太子春秋方始十歲，未見保傅九德之義，[四]宜簡賢輔，就成聖質。

translations.literal: [Three] Today the imperial heir is only just ten years old; he has not yet received the tutors' instruction in the Nine Virtues. [Four] Worthy assistants should be chosen to complete his sage-like nature.'
translations.idiomatic: Note [3]: The crown prince is a boy of ten who has not yet been taught the Nine Virtues. Note [4]: Select good men to mold his character.'"

sentences.zh: 」書奏不省。

translations.literal: 'The memorial was submitted but not acted on.
translations.idiomatic: The throne ignored the memorial.'

sentences.zh: 注[一]詳審而平當也。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: Careful scrutiny and balanced fitness.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: Xiang dang means his opinions were careful and sound.

sentences.zh: 注[二]趙人江充，字次倩。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: Jiang Chong of Zhao, styled Ciqian.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [2]: Jiang Chong of Zhao, style Ciqian.

sentences.zh: 武帝時，為直指繡衣，劾太子家吏行馳道中，恐為太子所誅，見上年老，意多所惡，因言左右皆為巫蠱。

translations.literal: Under Emperor Wu he served as straight-pointer embroidered-clothing inspector; he impeached the heir's household officials for driving in the reserved track, feared execution by the heir, saw the emperor advanced in years and disposed to suspicion, and therefore declared that everyone around the throne practiced gu sorcery.
translations.idiomatic: As an imperial inspector under Wu he denounced the heir's staff for using the imperial carriage road, then, fearing the heir's revenge and playing on the aging emperor's suspicions, spread tales of witchcraft throughout the court.

sentences.zh: 上乃使充捕案巫蠱。

translations.literal: The emperor thereupon sent Chong to arrest and investigate gu.
translations.idiomatic: Wudi ordered him to hunt for gu evidence.

sentences.zh: 既知上意太子，乃言宮中有蠱氣，遂掘蠱太子宮，得桐木人。

translations.literal: Once he knew the emperor's mind was set against the heir, he reported an aura of gu in the palace, dug for gu in the heir's quarters, and produced a wooden figurine of paulownia.
translations.idiomatic: Reading the emperor's intent, he claimed a witchcraft miasma in the palace, excavated the heir's residence, and 'found' a wooden doll.

sentences.zh: 時上疾在甘泉宮，太子懼，不能自明，收充斬之，發兵與丞相劉屈犛戰，敗，亡走湖，自殺。

translations.literal: The emperor was ill at Ganquan Palace; the heir, terrified and unable to clear himself, seized Chong and executed him, raised troops and fought the minister Liu Quli, was defeated, fled to Hu, and killed himself.
translations.idiomatic: Trapped at Ganquan, the heir killed Chong, fought Chancellor Liu Quli, lost, fled to Hu, and took his own life.

sentences.zh: 後太子孫宣帝即位，追謚太子曰戾，於湖置園邑奉祠，故曰戾園。

translations.literal: Later the heir's grandson Emperor Xuan ascended the throne, posthumously titled the heir as Li, established a park and town at Hu for sacrifices—hence the name Li Garden.
translations.idiomatic: Xuan, his grandson, later honored him posthumously as 'Li' and founded the Li Garden cult at Hu.

sentences.zh: 注[三]逮，及也。

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: Dai means 'to reach' or 'to catch up.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [3]: Dai here means 'in time to be of use.'

sentences.zh: 太子死後，壺關三老令狐茂上書訟太子冤，武帝感寤，憐太子無辜，乃族滅江充，作思子宮，為歸來望思之台於湖，天下聞而悲之。

translations.literal: After the heir's death the Sangu elder of Huguan, Linghu Mao, submitted a memorial pleading the heir's innocence; Emperor Wu was moved, pitied the heir as guiltless, exterminated Jiang Chong's clan, built the Palace for Pondering the Son, and erected the Terrace of Return and Longing at Hu; all under Heaven heard and grieved.
translations.idiomatic: When Linghu Mao of Huguan pleaded the dead heir's cause, Wu destroyed Jiang Chong's line, raised monuments of remorse at Hu, and the empire wept.

sentences.zh: 事見前書。

translations.literal: The affair is recorded in the Former Book.
translations.idiomatic: See the Han shu for the full story.

sentences.zh: 注[四]尚書戲繇陳九德，曰「寬而栗，柔而立，願而恭，亂而敬，擾而毅，直而溫，簡而廉，剛而塞，強而誼」也。

translations.literal: Commentary [four]: The Documents of Yu in the 'Xi and Yao' section lists the Nine Virtues, saying 'broad yet cautious, gentle yet firm, reverent yet respectful, orderly yet reverent, docile yet resolute, straightforward yet mild, detached yet incorrupt, bold yet principled, strong yet righteous.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [4]: The 'Nine Virtues' passage comes from the Shang shu's 'Counsels of Gao Yao.'

sentences.zh: 及順帝即位，拜□司空，在事多所薦達，天下稱其推士。

translations.literal: When Emperor Shun took the throne, [Zhang] was appointed grand commandant; in office he repeatedly recommended men for advancement, and the empire praised him for promoting scholars.
translations.idiomatic: Under Shun he became grand commandant and made a name recommending talent across the realm.

sentences.zh: 時清河趙騰上言災變，譏刺朝政，章下有司，收騰系考，所引黨輩八十餘人，皆以誹謗當伏重法。

translations.literal: At that time Zhao Teng of Qinghe submitted words on disasters and portents, satirizing court governance; the memorial was forwarded to the authorities, Teng was seized and interrogated, and more than eighty persons implicated in his faction were all judged guilty of slander meriting heavy punishment.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Teng of Qinghe criticized the government; the case snared over eighty men on capital charges of defamation.

sentences.zh: □上疏諫曰：「臣聞堯舜立敢諫之鼓，三王樹誹謗之木，春秋采善書惡，聖主不罪芻蕘。

translations.literal: [Zhang] submitted a remonstrance saying: 'I have heard that Yao and Shun set up a drum for bold remonstrance, the Three Kings planted a post for public criticism, the Spring and Autumn records good and censures evil, and a sage lord does not punish the ignorant.'
translations.idiomatic: Zhang answered: 'Yao and Shun welcomed outspoken advice; the sage kings did not punish plain speech; the Chunqiu honors truth.'

sentences.zh: [一]騰等雖幹上犯法，所言本欲盡忠正諫。

translations.literal: [One] Though Teng and the others violated the law in importuning the throne, their words aimed at utter loyalty and upright remonstrance.'
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: Teng's band broke the law by lecturing the throne, yet they meant only to speak truth to power.'

sentences.zh: 如當誅戮，天下杜口，塞諫爭之源，非所以昭德示後也。

translations.literal: 'If they are put to death, the empire will seal its lips and block the fountainhead of remonstrance—this is no way to display virtue for posterity.'
translations.idiomatic: Executing them would silence every critic and choke off honest counsel—hardly a lesson for later ages.'

sentences.zh: 」帝乃悟，減騰死罪一等，余皆司寇。

translations.literal: 'The emperor then understood, reduced Teng's crime from death by one degree, and sentenced the rest to penal servitude as foresters.'
translations.idiomatic: The emperor relented: Teng's death sentence was commuted one grade, the rest sentenced to two years as border guards.'

sentences.zh: [二]四年，以陰陽不和策免。

translations.literal: [Two] In the fourth year he was dismissed by edict because yin and yang were discordant.
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: In the fourth year he was cashiered, nominally for cosmic disharmony.

sentences.zh: 注[一]左氏傳曰：「春秋之稱，微而顯，志而晦，懲惡而勸善，非聖人誰能修之。」

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: The Zuo Zhuan states: 'The Spring and Autumn's phrasing is subtle yet manifest, records intent yet obscure, punishes evil and encourages good—who but a sage could compile it?'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: A line from the Zuo zhuan preface on the Chunqiu's moral diction.

sentences.zh: 注[二]前書音義曰：「司寇，二歲刑也。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: The Former Book's Sounds and Meanings state: 'Forester convict is a two-year sentence.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [2]: The Han shu commentary defines sikou labor as two years.

sentences.zh: 」輸作司寇，因以名焉。

translations.literal: 'They were transported to labor as foresters, hence the name.
translations.idiomatic: The convicts were called foresters after their corvée duty.'

sentences.zh: 陽嘉元年，復為廷尉。

translations.literal: In the first year of Yangjia he again became minister of justice.
translations.idiomatic: In 132 CE he was again named minister of justice.

sentences.zh: 其年卒官，時年八十三。

translations.literal: That year he died in office at the age of eighty-three.
translations.idiomatic: He died in post the same year, aged eighty-three.

sentences.zh: 遣使者弔祭，賜葬地於河南縣。

translations.literal: Envoys were dispatched to condole and sacrifice, and a burial plot in Henan county was granted.
translations.idiomatic: The court sent envoys to mourn and granted him a grave site in Henan county.

sentences.zh: 子綱。

translations.literal: His son was Gang.
translations.idiomatic: His son was Zhang Gang.

sentences.zh: 綱字文紀。

translations.literal: Gang styled Wenji.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Gang, styled Wenji.

sentences.zh: 少明經學。

translations.literal: In youth he was clear in classical learning.
translations.idiomatic: He mastered the classics while young.

sentences.zh: 雖為公子，而厲布衣之節。

translations.literal: Though a nobleman's son, he cultivated the integrity of a common cloth scholar.
translations.idiomatic: Though born to privilege, he lived like a poor scholar.

sentences.zh: 舉孝廉不就，司徒辟高第為*[侍]*御史。

translations.literal: He was recommended as filial and incorrupt but did not accept; the minister of education summoned him with high rank as *[attending]* imperial clerk.
translations.idiomatic: He declined nomination as filial and incorrupt; the minister of education then called him up with high standing as imperial clerk.

sentences.zh: 時順帝委縱宦官，有識危心。

translations.literal: At that time Emperor Shun indulged the eunuchs; thoughtful men were anxious at heart.
translations.idiomatic: Shun lavished favor on the palace attendants, and men of sense were alarmed.

sentences.zh: 綱常感激，慨然歎曰：「穢惡滿朝，不能奮身出命埽國家之難，雖生吾不願也。

translations.literal: Gang was often stirred in spirit and sighed with feeling: 'Vile corruption fills the court; if I cannot hazard my life to sweep away the state's peril, I do not wish to live.'
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Gang cried out inwardly: 'While the court reeks with filth, I would rather die than stand idle.'

sentences.zh: 」退而上書曰：「詩曰：『不愆不忘，率由舊章。

translations.literal: 'He withdrew and submitted a memorial saying: The Classic of Poetry says, "Do not err, do not forget—follow the ancient statutes."'
translations.idiomatic: He then wrote: 'The Odes say, Hold fast to the old pattern and do not stray.'"

sentences.zh: 』[一]尋大漢初隆，及中興之世，文、明二帝，德化尤盛。

translations.literal: '[One] Consider how at Han's first rise and again at its restoration, Emperors Wen and Ming practiced transformation through virtue to an outstanding degree.'
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: At the founding and again at the Guangwu restoration, Wen and Ming ruled chiefly through moral example.'

sentences.zh: 觀其理為，易循易見，但恭儉守節，約身尚德而已。

translations.literal: 'Look at their governance: easy to follow, easy to see—nothing but reverent frugality, guarding integrity, restraining the self, and honoring virtue.'
translations.idiomatic: Their government was transparent: modesty, self-restraint, and deference to virtue were enough.'

sentences.zh: 中官常侍不過兩人，近幸賞賜裁滿數金，惜費重人，故家給人足。

translations.literal: 'Palace attendants and constant companions numbered no more than two; favorites received gifts of only a few catties of gold; cherishing expense and valuing men, therefore households were supplied and the people sufficient.'
translations.idiomatic: A pair of eunuchs sufficed at court; gifts to favorites were trifling; frugality and respect for labor kept the realm prosperous.'

sentences.zh: 夷狄聞中國優富，任信道德，所以奸謀自消而和氣感應。

translations.literal: 'The barbarians heard that China was rich and at ease, trusted in its power and virtue, so wicked plots dissolved of themselves and harmonious qi responded.'
translations.idiomatic: Barbarians saw China's ease and good faith, and intrigue melted away before her moral sway.'

sentences.zh: 而頃者以來，不遵舊典，無功小人皆有官爵，富之驕之而復害之，非愛人重器，承天順道者也。

translations.literal: 'Yet in recent times the old canons are not followed; meritless petty men all hold office and rank; enriching them and indulging them only to harm them again—this is not the way to cherish men and honor regalia or to receive Heaven and follow the Way.'
translations.idiomatic: Lately the law of sparing honors has collapsed: upstarts win titles, grow arrogant, and ruin themselves—no way to steward the mandate or the symbols of rule.'

sentences.zh: [二]伏願陛下少留聖思，割損左右，以奉天心。

translations.literal: [Two] I humbly beg Your Majesty to pause in sage reflection and curtail those at your side in order to obey Heaven's mind.'
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: I beg you to trim the favorites at court and heed Heaven.'"

sentences.zh: 」書奏不省。

translations.literal: 'The memorial was submitted but not examined.
translations.idiomatic: Again the memorial went unanswered.'

sentences.zh: 注[一]詩大雅也。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: It is from the Greater Odes of the Classic of Poetry.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: The quotation is from the Greater Odes.

sentences.zh: 愆，過也。

translations.literal: Qian means fault.
translations.idiomatic: Qian: 'fault.'

sentences.zh: 率，循也。

translations.literal: Shuai means follow.
translations.idiomatic: Shuai: 'to follow.'

sentences.zh: 言成王令德，不過循用舊典之文。

translations.literal: It means King Cheng's excellent virtue consisted in nothing more than following the text of the old canons.
translations.idiomatic: King Cheng's perfection lay in cleaving to ancient precedent.

sentences.zh: 注[二]器謂車服也。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: Qi means chariots and robes.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [2]: 'Regalia' means chariots and robes of rank.

sentences.zh: 言無功小人不可妄授也。

translations.literal: It means meritless petty men must not be rashly invested.
translations.idiomatic: Worthless men must not be given such emblems lightly.

sentences.zh: 左傳曰「唯器與名不可以假人」也。

translations.literal: The Zuo Zhuan states: 'Only regalia and titles may not be lent to others.'
translations.idiomatic: The Zuo zhuan: regalia and titles are not to be trifled away.

sentences.zh: 漢安元年，選遣八使徇行風俗，皆耆儒知名，多歷顯位，[一]唯綱年少，官次最微。

translations.literal: In the first year of Han'an, eight envoys were chosen and dispatched to tour customs; all were aged scholars of renown who had mostly passed through eminent posts, [one] only Gang was young and his official rank the lowest.
translations.idiomatic: In 142 CE eight famous inspectors were sent on circuit; Zhang Gang alone was young and junior in rank.

sentences.zh: 餘人受命之部，而綱獨埋其車輪於洛陽都亭，曰：「豺狼當路，安問狐狸！」

translations.literal: The others received their orders and went to their circuits, but Gang alone buried his cart wheels at the metropolitan lodge of Luoyang, saying: 'When wolves block the road, why ask about foxes!'
translations.idiomatic: While his colleagues set out, Zhang Gang dug in his wheels at the capital gate and swore he would strike the great predators first.

sentences.zh: [二]遂奏曰：「大將軍冀，河南尹不疑，蒙外戚之援，荷國厚恩，以芻蕘之資，居阿衡之任，不能敷揚五教，翼贊日月，而專為封豕長蛇，肆其貪叨，[三]甘心好貨，縱恣無底，多樹諂諛，以害忠良。

translations.literal: [Two] Thereupon he memorialized: 'Grand General Ji and Governor of Henan Yin Bu Yi, relying on support from the outer kin, have received the state's deep favor; with the meager talent of chaff and grass they occupy the post of prime regulator; they cannot spread the Five Teachings or assist the two luminaries, but act only as great boar and long serpent, indulging their greed, [three] delighting in covetousness without limit, raising up flatterers everywhere and harming the loyal and good.'
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: He impeached Liang Ji and Yin Bu for abusing imperial favor, wallowing like boar and serpent, Note [3]: wallowing in greed without limit, raising flatterers, and destroying honest men.

sentences.zh: 誠天威所不赦，不辟所宜加也。

translations.literal: Truly what Heaven's majesty would not spare and what summary law should visit upon them.
translations.idiomatic: They deserve neither Heaven's mercy nor the law's indulgence.

sentences.zh: 謹條其無君之心十五事，斯皆臣子所切齒者也。

translations.literal: 'I respectfully list fifteen instances of their heart without a lord—all that every minister and son gnashes his teeth at.'
translations.idiomatic: He listed fifteen counts of disloyal conduct that every loyal subject detested.'

sentences.zh: 」[四]書御，京師震竦。

translations.literal: '[Four] When the memorial reached the throne, the capital trembled.'
translations.idiomatic: Note [4]: The capital shook when his words reached the emperor.'

sentences.zh: [五]時冀妹為皇后，內寵方盛，諸梁姻族滿朝，帝雖知綱言直，終不忍用。

translations.literal: [Five] At that time Ji's younger sister was empress; inner favor was at its height; all the Liang affines filled the court; though the emperor knew Gang's words were straight, in the end he could not bear to use them.
translations.idiomatic: Note [5]: Liang Ji's sister was empress; though the emperor knew Zhang Gang spoke truth, he could not act against the Liang clan.

sentences.zh: 注[一]周舉傳曰：「詔遣八使巡行風俗，同時俱拜，天下號曰『八俊』。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: Zhou Ju's biography states: 'An edict dispatched eight envoys to tour customs; they were all invested at once, and the empire called them the Eight Exemplars.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: Zhou Ju's biography lists the famous 'Eight Exemplars' mission.

sentences.zh: 刺史、二千石有臧罪者，驛馬上之，墨綬已下便收；

translations.literal: 'Governors and two-thousand-bushel officials guilty of corruption were to be reported by post horse; from black-ribbon rank downward they were to be arrested outright;
translations.idiomatic: Corrupt governors were to be rushed to the capital by courier; lower officials could be seized on the spot;

sentences.zh: 其有清勤忠惠宜表異者，狀聞。

translations.literal: 'those who were pure, diligent, loyal, and kind and deserved special commendation were to have their conduct reported.'
translations.idiomatic: the honest and diligent were to be reported for reward.'

sentences.zh: 」八使名見順帝紀。

translations.literal: 'The eight envoys' names appear in Emperor Shun's annals.'
translations.idiomatic: Their names are in Shun's basic annals.'

sentences.zh: 注[二]前書京兆督郵侯文之辭。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: A phrase from the Former Book, the metropolitan director of retainers's officer Wen of Jingzhao.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [2]: The wolf-and-fox line echoes the Han shu biography of the capital's investigator.

sentences.zh: 注[三]左傳申包胥曰「吳為封豕長蛇，薦食上國」也。

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: Shen Baoxu in the Zuo Zhuan says, 'Wu is a great boar and long serpent, repeatedly devouring the upper states.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [3]: The 'boar and serpent' image comes from Shen Baoxu in the Zuo zhuan.

sentences.zh: 注[四]左傳曰「有無君之心，而後動於惡」也。

translations.literal: Commentary [four]: The Zuo Zhuan states, 'They had a heart without a lord, and afterward moved to evil.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [4]: From the Zuo zhuan on disloyal intent.

sentences.zh: 前書鄒陽謂蓋侯王長君曰：「太后怫郁泣血，切齒側目於貴臣矣。」

translations.literal: The Former Book: Zou Yang said to Marquis of Gai Wang Changjun, 'The empress dowager is choked with grief and gnashes her teeth and casts sidelong glances at your noble ministers.'
translations.idiomatic: Zou Yang's warning to Wang Xin about the empress dowager's rage (Han shu).

sentences.zh: 注[五]御，進也。

translations.literal: Commentary [five]: Yu means to advance.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [5]: Yu means the memorial was presented.

sentences.zh: 時廣陵賊張嬰等觿數萬人，殺刺史、二千石，寇亂揚徐閒，積十餘年，朝廷不能討。

translations.literal: At that time the Guangling bandit Zhang Ying and others with several tens of thousands in girdle-pendants killed the governor and two-thousand-bushel officials, raided between Yang and Xu, had piled up more than ten years, and the court could not suppress them.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Ying's tens of thousands of rebels had plagued Yang and Xu for a decade, killing senior officials.

sentences.zh: 冀乃諷尚書，以綱為廣陵太守，因欲以事中之。

translations.literal: Ji thereupon indirectly instructed the masters of writing to appoint Gang as governor of Guangling, intending thereby to trap him with the affair.
translations.idiomatic: Liang Ji maneuvered to send Zhang Gang as governor of Guangling, hoping the rebellion would destroy him.

sentences.zh: 前遣郡守，率多求兵馬，綱獨請單車之職。

translations.literal: Previously when governors were sent they mostly requested troops and horses; Gang alone asked for a lone carriage's commission.
translations.idiomatic: Earlier governors had demanded troops; Zhang Gang asked only for a cart and himself.

sentences.zh: 既到，乃將吏卒十餘人，逕造嬰壘，以慰安之，求得與長老相見，申示國恩。

translations.literal: When he arrived he led a dozen or so clerks and soldiers, went straight to Ying's fortress, consoled and reassured them, sought a meeting with the elders, and declared the state's grace.
translations.idiomatic: He rode straight to the bandit camp with a handful of men, sought the headmen, and explained the emperor's goodwill.

sentences.zh: 嬰初大驚，既見綱誠信，乃出拜謁。

translations.literal: Ying was greatly alarmed at first; once he saw Gang's good faith he came out to bow.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Ying was stunned, then moved by Zhang Gang's sincerity and came out to submit.

sentences.zh: 綱延置上坐，問所疾苦。

translations.literal: Gang seated him in the seat of honor and asked what pained them.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Gang gave him the place of honor and asked what wrongs drove them to arms.

sentences.zh: 乃譬之曰：「前後二千石多肆貪暴，[一]故致公等懷憤相聚。

translations.literal: He then explained to them: 'Former and later two-thousand-bushel men mostly indulged greed and cruelty, [one] therefore you gentlemen harbored resentment and gathered together.'
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: He said, 'Your anger rose because past governors were cruel and corrupt.'

sentences.zh: 二千石信有罪矣，然為之者又非義也。

translations.literal: 'The two-thousand-bushel men were indeed guilty, yet what you did was also not righteous.'
translations.idiomatic: They deserved blame, but rebellion was still wrong.'

sentences.zh: 今主上仁聖，欲以文德服叛，故遣太守，思以爵祿相榮，不願以刑罰相加，今誠轉禍為福之時也。

translations.literal: 'Now the lord is benevolent and sage, wishing to subdue rebels through civil virtue; therefore he has sent a governor who thinks to honor you with rank and stipend and does not wish to add punishments—this is truly the hour to turn peril into blessing.'
translations.idiomatic: The throne means to win you by grace and titles, not by the headsman's axe—this is your chance to choose life.'

sentences.zh: 若聞義不服，天子赫然震怒，荊、楊、兗、豫大兵雲合，豈不危乎？

translations.literal: 'If you hear of righteousness and will not submit, when the Son of Heaven blazes in wrath the hosts of Jing, Yang, Yan, and Yu will mass like clouds—will you not be in peril?'
translations.idiomatic: Defy him, and armies from four provinces will crush you—do you weigh the odds?

sentences.zh: 若不料強弱，非明也；

translations.literal: 'If you do not reckon strength and weakness, that is not enlightenment;
translations.idiomatic: To ignore strength and weakness is folly;

sentences.zh: □善取惡，非智也；

translations.literal: '[Lacuna: graph for shě] good and take evil is not wisdom;
translations.idiomatic: To cast aside good and embrace evil is not wisdom;

sentences.zh: 去順效逆，非忠也；

translations.literal: 'to leave the compliant and imitate the rebellious is not loyalty;
translations.idiomatic: to abandon loyalty for treason is disloyal;

sentences.zh: 身絕血嗣，非孝也；

translations.literal: 'to cut off one's blood heirs is not filial;
translations.idiomatic: to leave no posterity is unfilial;

sentences.zh: [二]背正從邪，非直也；

translations.literal: [Two] to turn your back on the straight and follow the crooked is not integrity;
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: to choose crooked over straight is not integrity;

sentences.zh: 見義不為，非勇也：六者成敗之幾，利害所從，公其深計之。

translations.literal: 'to see righteousness and not act is not courage: these six are the pivots of success and failure, where benefit and harm turn—think deeply on them.'
translations.idiomatic: to shrink from duty is not courage—these six points decide your fate; weigh them well.'

sentences.zh: 」嬰聞，泣下，曰：「荒裔愚人，不能自通朝廷，不堪侵枉，遂復相聚偷生，若魚游釜中，喘息須臾閒耳。

translations.literal: Ying heard, wept, and said: "We foolish men of the wild border could not reach the court ourselves, could not bear oppression and wrong, and so gathered again to steal life—we are like fish swimming in a cauldron, breathing in a mere moment's space."
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Ying wept: "We are outlaws who could not bear local tyrants—we swim in a boiling pot, with only a breath of time left."

sentences.zh: 今聞明府之言，乃嬰等更生之*

translations.literal: Now hearing the enlightened governor's words is for us the dawn of*
translations.idiomatic: Hearing your words is our new dawn—

translations.literal: (morning)
translations.idiomatic: (the graph for 'morning')

translations.literal: **[chen]*.
translations.idiomatic: The graphs complete the word meaning daybreak (another dawn).'

sentences.zh: 既陷不義，實恐投兵之日，不免孥戮。

translations.literal: 'Having fallen into unrighteousness, I truly fear that on the day we lay down arms we will not escape execution of wives and children.'
translations.idiomatic: We fear that even if we surrender, our families will still be slaughtered.'

sentences.zh: 」綱約之以天地，誓之以日月，嬰深感悟，乃辭還營。

translations.literal: 'Gang bound them by Heaven and earth and swore by sun and moon; Ying was deeply moved, took leave, and returned to camp.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Gang swore great oaths; Zhang Ying, deeply moved, returned to his camp.

sentences.zh: 明日，將所部萬餘人與妻子面縛歸降。

translations.literal: The next day he led more than ten thousand of his following together with wives and children, bound themselves face-forward, and returned in submission.
translations.idiomatic: The next day he brought ten thousand followers and their families, hands tied behind backs, to surrender.

sentences.zh: 綱乃單車入嬰壘，大會，置酒為樂，散遣部觿，任從所之；

translations.literal: Gang thereupon drove his lone cart into Ying's fortress, held a great assembly, set out wine and made merry, dispersed and sent off the girdle-pendants of his following, letting them go where they would;
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Gang entered the camp alone, feasted the bandits, disbanded their host, and let every man go his way.

sentences.zh: 親為卜居宅，相田疇；

translations.literal: He personally for them divined dwelling sites and inspected field boundaries;
translations.idiomatic: He chose homesteads for them and surveyed their fields.

sentences.zh: [三]子弟欲為吏者，皆引召之。

translations.literal: [Three] for sons and younger brothers who wished to become clerks, he all summoned them.
translations.idiomatic: Note [3]: Young men who wanted office he enrolled as clerks.

sentences.zh: 人情悅服，南州晏然。

translations.literal: Human feelings were pleased and submitted; the southern provinces were tranquil.
translations.idiomatic: The people were won over and the south grew calm.

sentences.zh: 朝廷論功當封，梁冀遏絕，乃止。

translations.literal: The court deliberated on merit and he ought to have been enfeoffed, but Liang Ji blocked and cut it off, and it stopped.
translations.idiomatic: The court would have rewarded him, but Liang Ji quashed it.

sentences.zh: 天子嘉美，征欲擢用綱，而嬰等上書乞留，乃許之。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven praised him beautifully, summoned him intending to promote Gang for use, but Ying and others submitted a memorial begging to retain him, and thereupon it was permitted.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor wanted to recall Zhang Gang to the capital, but Zhang Ying's petition to keep him was granted.

sentences.zh: 注[一]二千石謂太守也。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: Two-thousand-bushel means the governor.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: The two-thousand-bushel officials are governors.

sentences.zh: 注[二]凡祭皆用牲，故曰血嗣。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: All sacrifices use victims; hence it is called blood heirs.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [2]: The phrase alludes to ancestral sacrifice with livestock.

sentences.zh: 注[三]相，視也。

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: Xiang means inspect.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [3]: Xiang means to survey.

sentences.zh: 田並畔曰疇。

translations.literal: Fields with shared borders are called chou.
translations.idiomatic: Chou are contiguous fields.

sentences.zh: 綱在郡一年，年四十六卒。

translations.literal: Gang was in the commandery one year and died at forty-six.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Gang governed Guangling one year and died at forty-six.

sentences.zh: 百姓老幼相攜，詣府赴哀者不可勝數。

translations.literal: Old and young among the common people, supporting one another, came to the headquarters to mourn—beyond counting.
translations.idiomatic: Countless mourners, young and old, flocked to his yamen.

sentences.zh: 綱自被疾，吏人鹹為祠祀祈福，皆言「千秋萬歲，何時復見此君」。

translations.literal: From the time Gang fell ill, clerks and people all made offerings and prayed for blessing, all saying, 'For a thousand autumns and myriad years, when shall we again see such a lord?'
translations.idiomatic: While he was ill, the people prayed at shrines for his life, saying they might never see his like again.

sentences.zh: 張嬰等五百餘人制服行喪，送到犍為，負土成墳。

translations.literal: Zhang Ying and more than five hundred others donned mourning and conducted the funeral, escorted him to Qianwei, and carried earth to complete the mound.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Ying and five hundred followers wore full mourning, escorted the body to Qianwei, and piled the grave mound with their own hands.

sentences.zh: 詔曰：「故廣陵太守張綱，大臣之苗，剖符統務，正身導下，班宣德信，降集劇賊張嬰萬人，息干戈之役，濟蒸庶之困，未升顯爵，不幸早卒。

translations.literal: An edict stated: 'The late governor of Guangling, Zhang Gang, was scion of great ministers, held the tally and directed affairs, corrected himself to guide subordinates, spread abroad virtuous trust, gathered in the fierce bandit Zhang Ying and his myriad men, stilled the labor of shields and spears, relieved the distress of the steaming multitude, had not yet been raised to eminent rank, and unluckily died early.'
translations.idiomatic: The edict praised Zhang Gang for pacifying tens of thousands of rebels without a major campaign, then mourned his early death before ennoblement.

sentences.zh: 嬰等縗杖，若喪考妣，朕甚愍焉！

translations.literal: Ying and others wore hemp and staffs as if mourning father and mother; I am deeply moved!'
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Ying mourned him like a parent; the emperor said he was deeply moved.

sentences.zh: 」拜綱子續為郎中，賜錢百萬。

translations.literal: Gang's son Xu was appointed gentleman of the palace and granted a million cash.
translations.idiomatic: He named Zhang Xu a palace gentleman and gave the family a million cash.

sentences.zh: 永建元年，征龔為太僕，轉太常。

translations.literal: In the first year of Yongjian, Gong was summoned as grand coachman, then transferred to grand master of ceremonies.
translations.idiomatic: In 126 CE Wang Gong became grand coachman, then grand master of ceremonies.

sentences.zh: 四年，遷司空，以地震策免。

translations.literal: In the fourth year he was promoted to grand commandant and dismissed by edict because of earthquake.
translations.idiomatic: In 129 CE he rose to grand commandant but was cashiered after an earthquake.

sentences.zh: 永和元年，拜太尉。

translations.literal: In the first year of Yonghe he was appointed grand commandant.
translations.idiomatic: In 136 CE he became grand commandant.

sentences.zh: 在位恭慎，自非公事，不通州郡書記。

translations.literal: In office he was respectful and cautious; except for public business he did not communicate with provincial clerks and letter-writers.
translations.idiomatic: He kept a clean line: no private letters from local officials.

sentences.zh: 其所辟命，皆海內長者。

translations.literal: Those whom he summoned and appointed were all venerable men within the seas.
translations.idiomatic: Everyone he appointed was a respected elder of the realm.

sentences.zh: 龔深疾宦官專權，志在匡正，乃上書極言其狀，請加放斥。

translations.literal: Gong deeply hated the eunuchs' monopoly of power; his intent was to rectify the state, and he submitted a memorial speaking to the limit of their condition, requesting they be banished.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Gong despised eunuch power and demanded their expulsion in blunt memorials.

sentences.zh: 諸黃門恐懼，各使賓客誣奏龔罪，順帝命亟自實。

translations.literal: The various Yellow Gates were afraid; each sent retainers to memorialize falsely Gong's crimes, and Emperor Shun ordered him urgently to verify himself.
translations.idiomatic: The eunuchs struck back with false charges; Shun ordered Wang Gong to answer under oath at once.

sentences.zh: [一]前掾李固時為大將軍梁商從事中郎，乃奏記於商曰：「今旦聞下太尉王公□令自實，未審其事深淺何如。

translations.literal: [One] Former clerk Li Gu at that time was staff supervisor to Grand General Liang Shang, and thereupon submitted a note to Shang saying: 'This morning I heard that the Grand Commandant Lord Wang [lacuna] was ordered to verify himself; I have not yet examined how deep or shallow the affair is.'
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: Li Gu, serving Liang Shang, wrote that the morning order for Wang Gong to clear himself was ominous.

sentences.zh: 王公束修厲節，敦樂蓺文，不求苟得，不為苟行，[二]但以堅貞之操，違俗失觿，橫為讒佞所構毀，觿人聞知，莫不歎栗。

translations.literal: Lord Wang cultivates himself with dried meat and strict integrity, delights earnestly in the arts and letters, does not seek careless gain, does not do careless conduct, [two] but because of his firm and pure integrity he offends custom and loses the girdle-pendants, is wantonly framed and slandered by flatterers—when the girdle-pendants hear of it, none do not sigh and tremble.
translations.idiomatic: Li Gu praised Wang Gong's austerity and learning, said his uprightness had made him enemies, and warned that honest men everywhere were horrified.

sentences.zh: 夫三公尊重，承天像極，未有詣理訴冤之義。

translations.literal: Now the three dukes are honored and weighty; they receive Heaven's image at the pole—there is no principle of going to a tribunal to plead grievance.
translations.idiomatic: The three dukes mirror the Three Stars; they should not be dragged into court like common suspects.

sentences.zh: [三]纖微感□，輒引分決，是以舊典不有大罪，不至重問。

translations.literal: [Three] For the slightest touch of stimulus [lacuna] they are made to draw lots and decide—therefore the old canons say that without great crime there is no reaching to heavy interrogation.
translations.idiomatic: Note [3]: Minor slights once forced grandees to verify themselves; ancient law reserved heavy inquiry for grave crimes only.

sentences.zh: [四]王公沈靜內明，不可加以非理。

translations.literal: [Four] Lord Wang is deep, still, inwardly perspicacious; he cannot be subjected to unreasonable treatment.
translations.idiomatic: Note [4]: Wang Gong is too principled to face such indignity.

sentences.zh: 卒有它變，則朝廷獲害賢之名，腢臣無救護之節矣。

translations.literal: If suddenly there is some other change, then the court will incur the name of harming the worthy, and fawning ministers will have no integrity of rescue and protection.
translations.idiomatic: If harm came to him, the throne would be shamed as killer of worthies and courtiers would have failed their duty to shield him.

sentences.zh: 昔絳侯得罪，袁盎解其過，[五]魏尚獲戾，馮唐訴其冤，[六]時君善之，列在書傳。

translations.literal: In former times Marquis of Jiang committed an offense and Yuan Ang explained his fault; [five] Wei Shang met blame and Feng Tang pleaded his wrong; [six] the lords of the time praised them—they are listed in documents and tradition.
translations.idiomatic: Note [5]: Yuan Ang saved Zhou Bo; Note [6]: Feng Tang pleaded for Wei Shang—both praised in the histories.

sentences.zh: 今將軍內倚至尊，外典國柄，言重信著，指撝無違，宜加表救，濟王公之艱難。

translations.literal: Now the general inwardly leans on the supreme, outwardly holds the state's handle; his words are weighty and his trust established—his directives are not disobeyed; you ought to memorialize in rescue and relieve Lord Wang's straits.
translations.idiomatic: Li Gu urged Liang Shang to use his immense influence to save Wang Gong.

sentences.zh: 語曰：『善人在患，饑不及餐。

translations.literal: The saying states: 'When a good man is in peril, he is too hungry to eat.'
translations.idiomatic: The proverb says a good man in crisis cannot pause for food.

sentences.zh: 』斯其時也。

translations.literal: This is that hour.
translations.idiomatic: That hour is now.

sentences.zh: 」商即言之於帝，事乃得釋。

translations.literal: Shang immediately spoke to the emperor, and the affair was then released.
translations.idiomatic: Liang Shang spoke to the emperor and the case was dropped.

sentences.zh: 注[一]亟，急也，音紀力反。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: Ji means urgent; the sound is ji li fan.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: Ji means urgently; read ji-li.

sentences.zh: 注[二]前書曰，楊子雲曰：「蜀嚴湛冥不作苟見，不為苟得。」

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: The Former Book states, Master Yang Yun said: 'Shu Yan Zhanming of Shu did not make careless display, did not seek careless gain.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [2]: Yang Xiong's praise of Yan Zun from the Han shu.

sentences.zh: 注[三]三公承助天子，位像三台，故曰承天像極。

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: The three dukes assist the Son of Heaven; their positions image the Three Terraces—hence they receive Heaven's image at the pole.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [3]: The three high ministers correspond to the three stars of Taisui.

sentences.zh: 哀帝時，丞相王嘉有罪，召詣廷尉詔獄。

translations.literal: In the time of Emperor Ai, Chancellor Wang Jia had committed a crime and was summoned to the edict prison of the minister of justice.
translations.idiomatic: Under Ai, Chancellor Wang Jia was called to the minister of justice's prison.

sentences.zh: 主簿曰「將相不對理陳冤，相踵以為故事，君侯宜引決」也。

translations.literal: The chief clerk said, 'Generals and ministers do not face judgment to state grievance; following one another they took it as precedent—my lord ought to draw the lot and decide.'
translations.idiomatic: His chief clerk cited precedent that high ministers do not plead in criminal court and urged suicide.

sentences.zh: 注[四]大臣獄重，故曰重問。

translations.literal: Commentary [four]: Great ministers' cases are weighty; hence they are called heavy interrogation.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [4]: Heavy interrogation means grave criminal inquiry of senior officials.

sentences.zh: 成帝時，丞相薛宣﹑御史大夫翟方進有罪，上使五二千石雜問。

translations.literal: In the time of Emperor Cheng, Chancellor Xue Yan and imperial counselor Zhai Fangjin had crimes; the emperor sent five two-thousand-bushel men jointly to interrogate.
translations.idiomatic: Chengdi used five senior officials to try Xue Yan and Zhai Fangjin.

sentences.zh: 音義云：「大獄重，故以二千石五人同問之。」

translations.literal: The Sounds and Meanings state: 'Great cases being weighty, therefore five two-thousand-bushel men jointly questioned them.'
translations.idiomatic: The commentary explains joint trial for major cases.

sentences.zh: 注[五]文帝時，丞相絳侯周勃免就國，人告以為反，諸公莫敢為言，唯郎中袁盎明絳侯無罪。

translations.literal: Commentary [five]: In the time of Emperor Wen, Marquis of Jiang Zhou Bo was dismissed and went to his state; men reported him as plotting rebellion; the various lords dared speak for none; only Gentleman Yuan Ang clarified that the Marquis of Jiang was guiltless.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [5]: Yuan Ang cleared Zhou Bo when he was accused of treason under Wen.

sentences.zh: 絳侯得釋，盎有力也。

translations.literal: The Marquis of Jiang obtained release; Ang had power therein.
translations.idiomatic: Yuan Ang secured Zhou Bo's release.

sentences.zh: 注[六]馮唐，安陵人，文帝時為郎署長。

translations.literal: Commentary [six]: Feng Tang, a man of Anling, was chief of the gentlemen's corps under Emperor Wen.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [6]: Feng Tang of Anling served Wendi.

sentences.zh: 上與論將帥，唐曰：「臣聞魏尚為雲中守，坐上功首虜差六級，陛下下之吏，削其爵，罰作之。

translations.literal: The emperor discoursed with him on generals and commanders; Tang said: 'I have heard that Wei Shang as governor of Yunzhong sat guilty because his merit tally of heads taken was short six grades; Your Majesty handed him to the clerks, stripped his rank, and sentenced him to labor.'
translations.idiomatic: Feng Tang cited Wei Shang's unjust demotion for a minor error in battle returns.

sentences.zh: 臣愚以為陛下法太明，罰太重。

translations.literal: Your servant foolishly thinks Your Majesty's laws are too bright and punishments too heavy.
translations.idiomatic: He told Wendi the law was too harsh.

sentences.zh: 」文帝悅，捨尚復官也。

translations.literal: Emperor Wen was pleased and pardoned Shang, restoring his office.
translations.idiomatic: Wendi restored Wei Shang.

sentences.zh: 龔在位五年，以老病乞骸骨，卒於家。

translations.literal: Gong was in office five years; because of old age and illness he begged his bones and died at home.
translations.idiomatic: After five years Wang Gong retired ill and died at home.

sentences.zh: 子暢。

translations.literal: His son was Chang.
translations.idiomatic: His son was Wang Chang.

sentences.zh: 論曰：張□﹑王龔，稱為*

translations.literal: The evaluation states: Zhang [lacuna in text] and Wang Gong were titled
translations.idiomatic: The historian praises Zhang Hao and Wang Gong as

translations.literal: (ya)
translations.idiomatic: the compound begins with the graph ya (refinement);

sentences.zh: **[推]*士，若其好通汲善，明發升薦，仁人之情也。

translations.literal: tui) scholars; if their delight in drawing from the well and raising the good, their clear-sighted promotion of the worthy, is the disposition of humane men.
translations.idiomatic: tui completes the epithet for men who drew up talent from obscurity—if such open-handed recommendation is the mark of the humane man.

sentences.zh: 夫士進則世收其器，賢用即人獻其能。

translations.literal: When scholars advance, the age gathers their vessels; when the worthy are employed, men present their abilities.
translations.idiomatic: When scholars rise, the state gains their tools; when worthies serve, men offer their skills.

sentences.zh: 能獻既已厚其功，器收亦理兼天下。

translations.literal: Once abilities are presented, their merit is already enriched; once vessels are gathered, order likewise embraces All under Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: Recognized talent repays with deeds; employed worthies bring order to the realm.

sentences.zh: [一]其利甚博，而人莫之先，豈同折枝於長者，以不為為難乎？

translations.literal: [One] The benefit is very broad, yet no one takes the lead—is it not like breaking a twig for an elder, taking 'not doing' as difficult?
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: The gain is vast, yet who takes the lead—as easy as breaking a twig for an elder, yet men pretend it is hard.

sentences.zh: [二]昔柳下惠見抑於臧文，[三]淳于長受稱於方進。

translations.literal: [Two] In former times Liuxiahui was suppressed by Zang Wen; [three] Chunyu Chang was praised by Fangjin.
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: Liuxiahui was blocked by Zang Wenzhong; Note [3]: Chunyu Chang was praised by Zhai Fangjin.

sentences.zh: [四]然則立德者以幽陋好遺，顯登者以貴塗易引。

translations.literal: [Four] Thus those who establish virtue are easily left in obscurity and lowliness; those who mount to glory easily draw followers from exalted paths.
translations.idiomatic: Note [4]: The obscure virtuous are forgotten; the powerful attract clients.

sentences.zh: 故晨門有抱關之夫，[五]柱下無朱文之軫也。

translations.literal: Therefore at the morning gate there is a man who holds the bar; [five] below the pillar there are no vermilion-patterned crossbeams of carriages.
translations.idiomatic: Note [5]: So a gatekeeper may hide a sage, while the court sees no carriages of humble worthies.

translations.literal: [Six]
translations.idiomatic: Note [6]: Footnote marker in the received text.

sentences.zh: 注[一]言賢人見用，則人競獻其所能。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: It means when worthies are employed, men compete to present what they can do.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: Employed worthies draw forth every talent.

sentences.zh: 但有能即獻，動必有功，功多賞厚，故言已厚其功。

translations.literal: But if there is ability then it is presented, movement necessarily has merit; merit abundant, rewards thick—therefore it is said their merit is already enriched.
translations.idiomatic: Each deed wins its reward—that is enriching merit.

sentences.zh: 有才器必被收用，用則海內蒙福，故曰理兼天下。

translations.literal: If there is talent and vessel they must be gathered and used; when used, the within-seas receives blessing—therefore it is said order embraces All under Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: Using talent brings order to the world.

sentences.zh: 注[二]以不為為難，言不之難也。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: Taking 'not doing' as difficult means it is not difficult for them.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [2]: The phrase ironically says men pretend inaction is hard.

sentences.zh: 謂進賢達士，同折枝之易，而不為之。

translations.literal: It means advancing worthies and promoting scholars is as easy as breaking a twig, yet they do not do it.
translations.idiomatic: Promoting talent is as easy as snapping a twig—yet rulers neglect it.

sentences.zh: 孟子謂齊宣王曰：「今恩足以及禽獸，而不能加於百姓者何？

translations.literal: Mencius said to King Xuan of Qi: 'Now your kindness suffices to reach birds and beasts, yet it cannot reach the common people—why?'
translations.idiomatic: Mencius asked Xuan why his kindness reached animals but not the people.

sentences.zh: 非力不能，是不為也。」

translations.literal: It is not that you lack strength—it is that you will not do it.
translations.idiomatic: The answer: not inability but unwillingness.

sentences.zh: 王曰：「不能不為，二者謂何也？

translations.literal: The king said: 'Not willing and not able—what of these two?'
translations.idiomatic: Xuan asked the difference between cannot and will not.

sentences.zh: 」孟子曰：「夫挾太山以超北海，王能乎？」

translations.literal: Mencius said: 'To clasp Mount Tai and leap over the North Sea—can Your Majesty do it?'
translations.idiomatic: Mencius: Can you leap the sea hugging Mount Tai?

sentences.zh: 王曰：「不能。

translations.literal: The king said: 'I cannot.'
translations.idiomatic: The king said no.

sentences.zh: 」「為長者折枝，王能乎？

translations.literal: To break a twig for an elder—can Your Majesty do it?
translations.idiomatic: To snap a twig for an elder—can you?

sentences.zh: 」曰：「不能也。

translations.literal: He said: 'I cannot.'
translations.idiomatic: The king still said he could not—though the act is trivial, he would not admit willingness.

sentences.zh: 」孟子曰：「夫挾太山以超*[北]*海，是實不能，不可強也。

translations.literal: Mencius said: 'To clasp Mount Tai and leap over the north sea—that is truly unable, not to be forced.
translations.idiomatic: Mencius: Hugging Tai to jump the sea is truly impossible.

sentences.zh: 為長者折枝甚易，而王不為，非不能也。

translations.literal: To break a twig for an elder is very easy, yet Your Majesty does not do it—it is not inability.
translations.idiomatic: Breaking a twig is trivial; refusing is choice, not incapacity.

sentences.zh: 老吾老，以及人之老，幼吾幼，以及人之幼，天下可運諸掌，何為不能加於百姓乎？

translations.literal: Treat my elders as I treat my own elders, treat my young as I treat my own young—all under Heaven may be turned in the palm; how is it that you cannot extend it to the common people?
translations.idiomatic: Care for others' kin as your own and the empire fits your hand—so why not extend kindness to the people?

sentences.zh: 」劉熙注孟子曰：「析枝，若今之案摩也。」

translations.literal: Liu Xi commenting on Mencius states: 'Breaking a twig is like today's massage at the table.'
translations.idiomatic: Liu Xi glosses zhezhi as massage—a trivial service.

sentences.zh: 注[三]柳下惠姓展，名禽，字獲，食邑於柳下，謚曰惠。

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: Liuxiahui's surname was Zhan, personal name Qin, style Huo; his food fief was below the willow; his posthumous title was Hui.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [3]: Liuxiahui was Zhan Qin, enfeoffed at Liuxia, posthumously Hui.

sentences.zh: 臧文仲，魯大夫，姓臧孫，名辰。

translations.literal: Zang Wenzhong was a grandee of Lu, surname Zangsun, personal name Chen.
translations.idiomatic: Zang Wenzhong was Zangsun Chen of Lu.

sentences.zh: 左傳仲尼曰：「臧文仲不仁者三，下展禽，廢六關，妾織蒲。

translations.literal: The Zuo Zhuan: Confucius said: 'Zang Wenzhong had three unkindnesses: he kept Liuxiahui in low place, abolished the six passes, had concubines weave rush mats.'
translations.idiomatic: Confucius listed Zang Wenzhong's three cruelties, including demoting Liuxiahui.

sentences.zh: 」言文仲知柳下惠之賢而使在下位，故曰抑之。

translations.literal: It means Wenzhong knew Liuxiahui's worthiness yet kept him in low rank—therefore it is called suppressing him.
translations.idiomatic: He knew Liuxiahui's worth yet left him low—that is suppression.

sentences.zh: 注[四]成帝時，定陵侯淳于長以太后姊子為九卿。

translations.literal: Commentary [four]: In the time of Emperor Cheng, Marquis of Dingling Chunyu Chang, as the empress dowager's elder sister's son, held one of the nine ministers.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [4]: Chunyu Chang, imperial in-law under Cheng, rose to nine ministers.

sentences.zh: 翟方進為丞相，獨與長交，稱薦之。

translations.literal: Zhai Fangjin was chancellor; alone he associated with Chang and praised and recommended him.
translations.idiomatic: Chancellor Zhai Fangjin alone befriended and praised Chunyu Chang.

sentences.zh: 注[五]論語：「子路宿於石門。

translations.literal: Commentary [five]: The Analects: 'Zilu lodged at Stone Gate.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [5]: From the Analects: Zilu at Stone Gate.

sentences.zh: 晨門曰：『奚自？

translations.literal: The morning gatekeeper said: 'Whence come you?'
translations.idiomatic: The gatekeeper asked where he came from.

sentences.zh: 』」注云：「石門，魯城外門也。

translations.literal: The commentary states: 'Stone Gate is the outer gate of Lu.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Stone Gate was Lu's outer gate.

sentences.zh: 晨，主守門，晨夜開閉也。

translations.literal: Chen means he guards the gate, opening and closing at dawn and night.
translations.idiomatic: Chen gatekeepers opened and closed at dawn and dusk.

sentences.zh: 」史記，侯嬴，夷門抱關者。

translations.literal: The Records of the Grand Historian: Hou Ying was the man who held the bar at Yimen.
translations.idiomatic: Sima Qian: Hou Ying held the bolt at Yimen.

sentences.zh: 守門必抱關，故兼言之。

translations.literal: Guarding the gate necessarily means holding the bar—therefore both are spoken together.
translations.idiomatic: Gate duty meant holding the bar—hence the paired phrase.

sentences.zh: 注[六]神仙傳曰：「老子，周宣王時為柱下史。

translations.literal: Commentary [six]: The Traditions of Divine Transcendents state: 'Laozi in the time of King Xuan of Zhou was scribe below the pillar.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [6]: The Shenxian zhuan puts Laozi as archivist under the Zhou pillar.

sentences.zh: 」朱文，畫車為文也。

translations.literal: Zhu wen means painting the carriage with patterns.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu wen: vermilion patterns on a carriage.

sentences.zh: 軫，車後橫木也。

translations.literal: Zhen is the rear crossbeam of a carriage.
translations.idiomatic: Zhen is the rear crossbar of a chariot.

sentences.zh: 言貧賤之人，多被淪□，所以晨門之下必有抱關之賢，柱下之微永無朱文之轍也。

translations.literal: It means men of poverty and lowliness are mostly sunk in obscurity [lacuna]; therefore below the morning gate there must be worthies who hold the bar, while in the lowliness below the pillar there are never tracks of vermilion-patterned crossbeams.
translations.idiomatic: The humble hide talent at the gate; the court never sends its painted carriages for them.

sentences.zh: 暢字叔茂。

translations.literal: Chang styled Shumao.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Chang, styled Shumao.

sentences.zh: 少以清實為稱，無所交黨。

translations.literal: In youth he was praised for purity and substance and joined no faction.
translations.idiomatic: He was known in youth for integrity and kept aloof from cliques.

sentences.zh: 初舉孝廉，辭病不就。

translations.literal: At first he was recommended as filial and incorrupt but declined on grounds of illness and did not take office.
translations.idiomatic: He declined his first nomination as filial and incorrupt, pleading illness.

sentences.zh: 大將軍梁商特辟舉茂才，四遷尚書令，出為齊相。

translations.literal: Grand General Liang Shang specially summoned him and recommended him as flourishing talent; he was promoted four times to director of the masters of writing and went out as chancellor of Qi.
translations.idiomatic: Liang Shang singled him out as maocai; he rose to director of the masters of writing, then chancellor of Qi.

sentences.zh: [一]征拜司隸校尉，轉漁陽太守。

translations.literal: [One] He was summoned as colonel director of retainers, then transferred to governor of Yuyang.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: He became colonel director of retainers, then governor of Yuyang.

sentences.zh: 所在以嚴明為稱。

translations.literal: Wherever he was, he was praised for severity and clarity.
translations.idiomatic: Every post won him a reputation for firm, clear rule.

sentences.zh: 坐事免官。

translations.literal: He sat an affair and was dismissed from office.
translations.idiomatic: He was later removed for an offense.

sentences.zh: 是時政事多歸尚書，桓帝特詔三公，令高選庸能。

translations.literal: At this time most government affairs fell to the masters of writing; Emperor Huan specially edicted the three dukes to order high selection of meritorious and able men.
translations.idiomatic: Power had shifted to the masters of writing; Huan ordered the three dukes to pick only the ablest men.

sentences.zh: [二]太尉陳蕃薦暢清方公正，有不可犯之色，[三]由是復為尚書。

translations.literal: [Two] Grand Commandant Chen Fan recommended Chang as pure, upright, and impartial, with a bearing that could not be violated, [three] whereupon he again became a master of writing.
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: Chen Fan praised Wang Chang's integrity; Note [3]: he was reappointed to the masters of writing.

sentences.zh: 注[一]齊王喜之相。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: He was chancellor to Prince Xi of Qi.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: Wang Chang had been chancellor to the Prince of Qi.

sentences.zh: 注[二]庸，功也。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: Yong means merit.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [2]: Yong here means 'merit' or 'capability.'

sentences.zh: 注[三]禮記曰：「介冑之士，則有不可犯之色。」

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: The Record of Rites states: 'Men in armor have a bearing that cannot be violated.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [3]: From the Liji on the demeanor of armored soldiers.

sentences.zh: 尋拜南陽太守。

translations.literal: Soon he was appointed governor of Nanyang.
translations.idiomatic: He was soon named governor of Nanyang.

sentences.zh: 前後二千石逼懼帝鄉貴戚，多不稱職。

translations.literal: Former and later two-thousand-bushel men, pressed and fearful of the emperor's home region's honored kin, mostly did not match their posts.
translations.idiomatic: Past governors had kowtowed to Luoyang grandees and failed in their duty.

sentences.zh: 暢深疾之，下車奮厲威猛，其豪黨有釁穢者，莫不糾發。

translations.literal: Chang deeply hated this; as soon as he descended from his carriage he roused fierce severity; among powerful factions who had guilt and filth, none were not exposed.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Chang arrived fierce: every powerful clan with dirty secrets he dragged into the light.

sentences.zh: 會赦，事得散。

translations.literal: It happened that there was an amnesty and the affairs were dispersed.
translations.idiomatic: A general amnesty then scattered the cases.

sentences.zh: 暢追恨之，更為設法，諸受臧二千萬以上不自首實者，盡入財物；

translations.literal: Chang pursued hatred of it and further established methods: all who had received bribes of twenty million or more and did not voluntarily confess the truth were to forfeit all property;
translations.idiomatic: He then ruled that embezzlers over twenty million who did not confess must forfeit everything;

sentences.zh: 若其隱伏，使吏發屋伐樹，堙井夷醋，豪右大震。

translations.literal: if they hid, he caused clerks to tear off roofs, cut down trees, fill wells, and level the pickling vats—the great families on the right were greatly shaken.
translations.idiomatic: if they hid assets, his men tore up houses, felled trees, filled wells, and smashed hidden stores—terrifying the magnates.

sentences.zh: 功曹張敞奏記諫曰：「五教在寬，著之經典。

translations.literal: Merit clerk Zhang Shang submitted a note remonstrating: 'The Five Teachings lie in leniency—it is written in the classics.
translations.idiomatic: His aide Zhang Shang urged: leniency is the root of the Five Teachings.

sentences.zh: 湯去三面，八方歸仁。

translations.literal: Tang removed three sides of the net and the eight directions returned to benevolence.
translations.idiomatic: King Tang opened three sides of the hunting net and won the realm's hearts.

sentences.zh: [一]武王入殷，先去炮格之刑。

translations.literal: [One] When King Wu entered Yin, he first abolished the paoge torture.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: King Wu ended Zhou's paoge torture.

sentences.zh: [二]高祖鑒秦，唯定三章之法。

translations.literal: [Two] The High Founder took Qin as mirror and fixed only the three-chapter laws.
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: Gaozu simplified Qin's laws to three chapters.

sentences.zh: 孝文皇帝感一緹縈，蠲除肉刑。

translations.literal: Emperor Wen of Filial Piety was moved by one girl Tiying and abolished mutilating punishments.
translations.idiomatic: Wendi abolished mutilation for Tiying's plea.

sentences.zh: [三]卓茂﹑文翁﹑召父之徒，皆疾惡嚴刻，務崇溫厚。

translations.literal: [Three] Men such as Zhuo Mao, Wen Weng, and Father Shao all hated cruelty and severity and strove to honor warmth and generosity.
translations.idiomatic: Note [3]: Zhuo Mao, Wen Weng, and Shaofu ruled gently, not harshly.

sentences.zh: [四] 仁賢之政，流聞後世。

translations.literal: [Four] The government of benevolent worthies flowed in report to later generations.
translations.idiomatic: Note [4]: Their mild rule was remembered for ages.

sentences.zh: 夫明哲之君，網漏吞舟之魚，[五]然後三光明於上，人物悅於下。

translations.literal: As for a perspicacious lord, the net leaks fish that could swallow a boat—[five] only then do the three luminaries shine above and creatures rejoice below.
translations.idiomatic: Note [5]: A wise ruler's net is loose enough for great fish—then sun, moon, and stars shine clear above and the people thrive below.

sentences.zh: 言之若迂，其效甚近。

translations.literal: Spoken, it seems remote; its effect is very near.
translations.idiomatic: The policy sounds lofty but works at once.

sentences.zh: [六]發屋伐樹，將為嚴烈，雖欲懲惡，難以聞遠。

translations.literal: [Six] To tear off roofs and cut trees will be severe and violent; though wishing to punish evil, it is hard to win distant report.
translations.idiomatic: Note [6]: House-breaking terror wins no lasting reputation.

sentences.zh: 以明府上智之才，日月之曜，[七]敷仁惠之政，則海內改觀，實有折枝之易，而無挾山之難。

translations.literal: With the enlightened governor's supreme talent and the radiance of sun and moon, [seven] if you spread benevolent and kindly government, then within the seas views will change—it truly has the ease of breaking a twig, not the difficulty of clasping a mountain.
translations.idiomatic: Note [7]: With your gifts, kindness would change the empire as easily as snapping a twig.

sentences.zh: 郡為舊都侯甸之國，園廟出於章陵，[八]三後生自新野，[九]士女沾教化，黔首仰風流，自中興以來，功臣將相，繼世而隆。

translations.literal: The commandery is the old capital's hou and dian region; the park temples come forth from Zhangling; [eight] the three empresses were born in Xinye; [nine] gentlemen and ladies have soaked in transformation, black-haired people look up to the current—since the restoration, merit ministers and generals have succeeded one another in eminence.
translations.idiomatic: Note [8–9]: Nanyang was the imperial heartland—tombs at Zhangling, empresses from Xinye—so harsh rule ill fits this soil.

sentences.zh: 愚以為懇懇用刑，不如行恩；

translations.literal: Your servant thinks earnestly using punishments is not as good as practicing grace;
translations.idiomatic: Earnest severity cannot match grace;

sentences.zh: 孳孳求奸，未若禮賢。

translations.literal: diligently seeking treachery is not as good as treating the worthy with ritual.
translations.idiomatic: zealous witch-hunts cannot match honoring worthies.

sentences.zh: 舜舉戲陶，不仁者遠。

translations.literal: When Shun raised Gao Yao, the unkindly went far away.
translations.idiomatic: When Shun raised Gao Yao, the cruel fled the court.

sentences.zh: [一０]隨會為政，晉盜奔秦。

translations.literal: [Ten] When Sui Hui directed policy, Jin's robbers fled to Qin.
translations.idiomatic: Note [10]: Under Sui Hui, Jin's thieves ran to Qin.

sentences.zh: [一一]虞﹑芮入境，讓心自生。

translations.literal: [Eleven] When Yu and Rui entered the border, yielding hearts arose of themselves.
translations.idiomatic: Note [11]: Yu and Rui were shamed into peace by Zhou's manners.

sentences.zh: [一二]化人在德，不在用刑。

translations.literal: [Twelve] Transforming people lies in virtue, not in using punishments.
translations.idiomatic: Note [12]: Moral example, not the rod, changes men.

sentences.zh: 」暢深納敞諫，更崇寬政，慎刑簡罰，教化遂行。

translations.literal: Chang deeply accepted Shang's remonstrance, further honored lenient government, was careful in punishments and simplified fines, and transformation then proceeded.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Chang took the advice, softened his rule, and Nanyang flourished.

sentences.zh: 注[一]史記曰，湯為夏方伯，得專征伐。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: The Records of the Grand Historian state: Tang was regional lord of Xia for Fangbo and could levy expeditions.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: Shiji: Tang as regional lord could wage war.

sentences.zh: 出見野張四面網，祝曰：「自天下四方，皆入吾網。

translations.literal: Going out he saw in the wild a net spread on four sides; the prayer said: 'From all under Heaven and the four directions, all enter my net.'
translations.idiomatic: He found a four-sided hunt with a prayer to catch everything.

sentences.zh: 」湯曰：「嘻，盡之矣！

translations.literal: Tang said: 'Alas, it is exhausted!'
translations.idiomatic: Tang cried, 'You would take all!'

sentences.zh: 去其三面！

translations.literal: Remove its three sides!'
translations.idiomatic: He ordered three sides torn away.'

sentences.zh: 」祝曰：「欲左左，欲右右，不用命，乃入吾網。

translations.literal: The prayer said: 'Those who wish left, left; those who wish right, right; whoever disobeys the command, then enters my net.'
translations.idiomatic: Birds might flee left or right; only the willful were caught.'

sentences.zh: 」諸侯聞曰：「湯德至禽獸！

translations.literal: The regional lords heard and said: 'Tang's virtue reaches even birds and beasts!'
translations.idiomatic: The lords marveled that Tang's virtue touched beasts.'

sentences.zh: 」於是諸侯畢服。

translations.literal: Thereupon the regional lords all submitted.
translations.idiomatic: They all submitted to Tang.'

sentences.zh: 嘻音僖。

translations.literal: Xi has the sound xi.
translations.idiomatic: Xi is read like xi.

sentences.zh: 注[二]列女傳：「紂為銅柱，以膏塗之，加於炭之上，使有罪緣焉，足滑跌墯，紂與妲己笑以為樂，名曰炮格之刑。

translations.literal: Commentary [two] Biographies of Exemplary Women: 'Zhou made bronze pillars, smeared them with fat, set them over coals, and made the guilty climb them; feet slipped and they fell; Zhou and Daji laughed for amusement—this was called the paoge torture.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [2]: The paoge torture from the Lienu zhuan.

sentences.zh: 」臣賢案：史記及帝王代紀皆言文王為西伯，獻洛西之地，請除炮格之刑。

translations.literal: Your servant Xian notes: the Records of the Grand Historian and the Dynastic Records of Emperors both say King Wen as Western Earl offered the land west of Luo and requested removal of the paoge torture.
translations.idiomatic: Li Xian: Shiji and imperial records credit King Wen, not Wu, with ending paoge.

sentences.zh: 今雲武王，與此不同。

translations.literal: Now it says King Wu—this differs from that.
translations.idiomatic: The text here says Wu, which disagrees.

sentences.zh: 注[三]文帝時，太倉令淳于公有罪當刑。

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: In Emperor Wen's time, Grand Provisioner Director Chunyu Yi was guilty and should have suffered mutilation.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [3]: Chunyu Yi faced mutilation under Wendi.

sentences.zh: 淳于公無男，有五女，罵其女曰：「生女不生男，緩急非有益也。

translations.literal: Director Chunyu had no sons but five daughters; he reviled his daughters saying: 'To bear daughters and not sons—at urgent moments they are of no use.'
translations.idiomatic: He cursed his daughters for being useless in a crisis.

sentences.zh: 」其少女緹縈自傷悲泣，隨父至長安，上書請沒官為婢以贖父。

translations.literal: His youngest daughter Tiying wounded herself weeping, followed her father to Chang'an, and submitted a memorial offering to become a bondmaid in official service to ransom her father.
translations.idiomatic: Tiying followed him to the capital and offered herself as a slave to ransom him.

sentences.zh: 文帝悲憐其意，為除肉刑。

translations.literal: Emperor Wen pitied her intent and for her sake abolished mutilating punishments.
translations.idiomatic: Wendi moved by her plea ended mutilation.

sentences.zh: 注[四]景帝時，文翁為蜀郡守，仁愛教化。

translations.literal: Commentary [four]: In Emperor Jing's time, Wen Weng was governor of Shu commandery, benevolent and loving in transformation.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [4]: Wen Weng's humane rule in Shu under Jing.

sentences.zh: 宣帝時，召信臣為南陽太守，視人如子，其化大行。

translations.literal: In Emperor Xuan's time, Zhao Xinchen was governor of Nanyang, looked on people as children, and his transformation greatly flourished.
translations.idiomatic: Under Xuandi, Shaofu fathered the people of Nanyang like children.

sentences.zh: 注[五]韓詩外傳曰：「夫吞舟之魚，不居潛澤。

translations.literal: Commentary [five]: Han Shi Waizhuan states: 'Fish that could swallow a boat do not dwell in shallow marshes.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [5]: Han shi waizhuan on great fish.

sentences.zh: 」前書曰「高祖約法三章，號為網漏吞舟之魚」也。

translations.literal: The Former Book states: 'The High Founder fixed the three-chapter laws and was called a net that leaks fish that could swallow a boat.'
translations.idiomatic: Han shu: Gaozu's loose net metaphor.

sentences.zh: 注[六]迂，遠也。

translations.literal: Commentary [six]: Yu means remote.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [6]: Yu means 'distant' or 'circuitous.'

sentences.zh: 注[七]莊子曰「飾智以驚愚，修身以明□，昭昭乎若揭日月而行」也。

translations.literal: Commentary [seven]: Zhuangzi states: 'They adorn wisdom to startle the foolish, cultivate the body to display [lacuna]—brightly as if carrying sun and moon while walking.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [7]: Zhuangzi on ostentatious virtue—lacuna in text.

sentences.zh: 注[八]五百里甸服，千里侯服。

translations.literal: Commentary [eight]: Five hundred li is dian service, a thousand li is hou service.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [8]: The Yugong system of hou and dian zones.

sentences.zh: 南陽去洛千里，故曰侯甸。

translations.literal: Nanyang is a thousand li from Luo—hence it is called hou and dian.
translations.idiomatic: Nanyang lay a thousand li from Luoyang, hence 'hou-dian' heartland.

sentences.zh: 南頓君以上四廟在焉。

translations.literal: The four temples from Lord Nandun upward are there.
translations.idiomatic: The ancestral shrines from Nandun upward stood there.

sentences.zh: 注[九]光烈皇后，和帝陰後﹑鄧後，並新野人。

translations.literal: Commentary [nine]: Empress Guanglie, Empress Yin of He, and Empress Deng were all natives of Xinye.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [9]: Guangwu's empress and later empresses came from Xinye.

sentences.zh: 注[一０]論語子夏之辭也。

translations.literal: Commentary [ten]: It is a phrase from Zi Xia in the Analects.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [10]: A line from the Analects, Zi Xia.

sentences.zh: 注[一一]左傳，晉命隨會將中軍，且為太傅，晉國之盜奔秦也。

translations.literal: Commentary [eleven]: The Zuo Zhuan: Jin ordered Sui Hui to command the center army and also be grand tutor; Jin's robbers fled to Qin.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [11]: Zuo zhuan on Sui Hui and fleeing thieves.

sentences.zh: 注[一二]史記曰，文王為西伯，陰行善化，諸侯皆來決平。

translations.literal: Commentary [twelve]: The Records of the Grand Historian states: King Wen as Western Earl secretly practiced good transformation; the regional lords all came to seek judgment.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [12]: Shiji on King Wen's hidden virtue.

sentences.zh: 於是虞﹑芮之人有獄不決，乃如周。

translations.literal: Thereupon men of Yu and Rui who had a suit they could not decide went to Zhou.
translations.idiomatic: Yu and Rai brought a lawsuit to Zhou.

sentences.zh: 入界，見耕者讓畔，少者讓長。

translations.literal: Entering the border they saw plowmen yield field boundaries and youths yield elders.
translations.idiomatic: They saw farmers yield boundary strips and youths defer to elders.

sentences.zh: 虞﹑芮二人不見西伯，籩而相謂曰：「吾所爭，周人所恥，曷為取辱？

translations.literal: The two men of Yu and Rui did not see the Western Earl; ashamed they said to each other: 'What we wrangle over, Zhou people would be ashamed of—why seek humiliation?'
translations.idiomatic: Ashamed to air petty quarrels before such manners, they said:

sentences.zh: 」遂俱讓而還也。

translations.literal: Thereupon they both yielded and returned.
translations.idiomatic: They went home and dropped the suit.

sentences.zh: 郡中豪族多以奢靡相尚，暢常布衣皮褥，車馬羸敗，以矯其敝。

translations.literal: Powerful clans in the commandery mostly vied in luxury; Chang constantly wore common cloth and a leather quilt, his carriage and horses lean and broken, thereby to correct their excess.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Chang wore plain cloth and drove a ragged cart to shame the rich.

sentences.zh: 同郡劉表時年十七，從暢受學。

translations.literal: Liu Biao of the same commandery was seventeen sui and followed Chang to receive study.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Biao, seventeen, studied under him.

sentences.zh: 進諫曰：「夫奢不僭上，儉不逼下，[一]循道行禮，貴處可否之閒。

translations.literal: He advanced remonstrance saying: 'Luxury does not transgress those above; austerity does not press those below; [one] following the Way and practicing ritual, value dwelling between what is permitted and what is not.'
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: Liu Biao urged balance: neither flaunting wealth nor flaunting poverty.

sentences.zh: 蘧伯玉恥獨為君子。

translations.literal: Qu Boyu was ashamed to be alone a gentleman.
translations.idiomatic: Qu Boyu was ashamed to be the only gentleman.

sentences.zh: 府君不希孔聖之明訓，而慕夷齊之末操，[二]無乃皎然自貴於世乎？

translations.literal: My lord does not hope for Confucius's clear instruction but admires the minor conduct of Yi and Qi—[two] is this not to shine in solitary pride before the world?'
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: You imitate Bo Yi rather than Confucius' middle way—are you not posing as holier than the world?'

sentences.zh: 」暢曰：「昔公儀休在魯，拔園葵，去織婦；

translations.literal: Chang said: 'In former times Gongyi Xiu in Lu pulled up the garden mallows and dismissed the weaving women;'
translations.idiomatic: Wang Chang answered with Gongyi Xiu, who tore out his profit-making garden.

sentences.zh: [三]孫叔敖相楚，其子被裘刈薪。

translations.literal: [Three] Sunshu Ao was chancellor of Chu; his son wore sheepskin and cut firewood.
translations.idiomatic: Note [3]: Sunshu Ao's son dressed poor.

sentences.zh: [四]夫以約失之鮮矣。

translations.literal: [Four] To err on the side of austerity is seldom a fault.
translations.idiomatic: Note [4]: Few err by being too spare.

sentences.zh: [五]聞伯夷之風者，貪夫廉，懦夫有立志。

translations.literal: [Five] Those who hear of Bo Yi's wind—greedy men grow pure, timid men set firm resolve.'
translations.idiomatic: Note [5]: Mencius on Bo Yi's transforming power.'

sentences.zh: [六]雖以不德，敢慕遺烈。」

translations.literal: [Six] Though lacking in virtue, I dare admire their vestiges.'
translations.idiomatic: Note [6]: I am unworthy, yet I follow their trace.'

sentences.zh: 注[一]禮記曰「君子上不僭上，下不逼下」也。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: The Record of Rites states: 'The gentleman does not transgress those above nor press those below.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: Liji on proper gradation.

sentences.zh: 注[二]論語孔子曰：「奢則不遜，儉則固。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: The Analects: Confucius said: 'Luxury leads to insubordination; austerity leads to meanness.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [2]: Confucius on luxury and austerity.

sentences.zh: 」言仲尼得奢儉之中，而夷齊饑死，是末操也。

translations.literal: It means Confucius obtained the mean between luxury and austerity, while Yi and Qi starved to death—that is minor conduct.
translations.idiomatic: Confucius held the mean; Bo Yi died for a 'minor' scruple.

sentences.zh: 注[三]史記曰，魯相公儀休之其家，見織帛，怒而出其婦，食於捨而茹葵，慍而拔其葵，曰：「吾已食祿，又奪園夫女子利乎？」

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: The Records of the Grand Historian state: Lu chancellor Gongyi Xiu went to his home, saw silk weaving, angrily expelled his wife; eating in his lodging he chewed mallows, was vexed and pulled up the mallows, saying: 'I already eat stipend—shall I again seize the profit of gardeners and weaving women?'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [3]: Shiji on Gongyi Xiu's integrity.

sentences.zh: 注[四]史記曰，孫叔敖為楚相，且死，屬其子曰：「我死，汝貧困，往見優孟，言孫叔敖子也。

translations.literal: Commentary [four]: The Records of the Grand Historian state: Sunshu Ao was chancellor of Chu; when about to die he instructed his son: 'When I die you will be poor; go see Actor Meng and say you are Sunshu Ao's son.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [4]: Sunshu Ao's deathbed advice.

sentences.zh: 」居數年，其子貧，負薪逢優孟。

translations.literal: After several years his son was poor, carried firewood, and met Actor Meng.
translations.idiomatic: Years later the son, poor, met Actor Meng while hauling fuel.

sentences.zh: 優孟言之於王，封之寢丘四百戶也。

translations.literal: Actor Meng spoke to the king and he was enfeoffed at Qinqiu with four hundred households.
translations.idiomatic: Meng won him a fief at Qinqiu.

sentences.zh: 注[五]論語孔子之辭也。

translations.literal: Commentary [five]: It is a phrase from Confucius in the Analects.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [5]: From the Analects.

sentences.zh: 言儉則無失。

translations.literal: It means austerity then has no fault.
translations.idiomatic: Austerity rarely errs.

sentences.zh: 注[六]孟子之辭。

translations.literal: Commentary [six]: A phrase from Mencius.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [6]: From Mencius.

sentences.zh: 後征為長樂□尉。

translations.literal: Later he was summoned as captain of [lacuna] at Changle.
translations.idiomatic: He was later recalled as a captain at Changle—lacuna in the office name.

sentences.zh: 建寧元年，遷司空，數月，以水災策免。

translations.literal: In the first year of Jianning he was promoted to grand commandant; within several months he was dismissed by edict because of flood.
translations.idiomatic: In 168 CE he became grand commandant but lost the post months later after floods.

sentences.zh: 明年，卒於家。

translations.literal: The next year he died at home.
translations.idiomatic: He died at home the next year.

sentences.zh: 子謙，為大將軍何進長史。

translations.literal: His son was Qian, who was chief clerk to Grand General He Jin.
translations.idiomatic: His son Wang Qian served He Jin as chief clerk.

sentences.zh: 謙子粲，以文才知名。

translations.literal: Qian's son Can was famous for literary talent.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Can, Qian's son, was famed for letters.

translations.literal: [One]
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: (marker)

sentences.zh: 注[一]粲字仲宣。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: Can styled Zhongxuan.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: Wang Can, style Zhongxuan.

sentences.zh: 蔡邕見而奇之。

translations.literal: Cai Yong saw him and marveled.
translations.idiomatic: Cai Yong was astonished at the boy.

sentences.zh: 時邕才學顯著，貴重朝廷，車騎填門，賓客盈坐。

translations.literal: At that time Yong's talent and learning were illustrious, honored and weighty at court; chariots and riders filled his gate, guests filled his seats.
translations.idiomatic: Cai Yong was then the capital's literary lion, his house mobbed.

sentences.zh: 聞粲在門，倒屣迎之。

translations.literal: Hearing Can was at the gate, he put his slippers on reversed to welcome him.
translations.idiomatic: Hearing Wang Can had come, Cai Yong ran out with his slippers on backward.

sentences.zh: 既至，年幼，容狀短小，一座盡驚。

translations.literal: When he arrived, he was young in years, short in stature and appearance, and the whole company was startled.
translations.idiomatic: The company was shocked—he was a mere child.

sentences.zh: 邕曰：「王公之孫，有異才，吾不如也。

translations.literal: Yong said: 'He is a grandson of the Wang house with extraordinary talent—I am not his match.'
translations.idiomatic: Cai Yong said the boy outshone him.

sentences.zh: 」太祖辟粲為丞相掾，後為侍中。
