translations.literal: The Grand Progenitor summoned Can as clerk to the chancellor, later making him palace attendant.
translations.idiomatic: Cao Cao made him chancellor's clerk, then palace attendant.

sentences.zh: 博物多識，問無不對。

translations.literal: Broad in learning and knowledge, there was no question to which he did not answer.
translations.idiomatic: He answered every question put to him.

sentences.zh: 嘗與人行，讀道邊碑，人問「卿能闇記乎」？

translations.literal: Once while walking with others he read a stele by the road; someone asked, 'Can you memorize it in the dark?'
translations.idiomatic: Once, after reading a roadside stele, he was asked to recite it from memory.

sentences.zh: 因使背而誦之，一文不失。

translations.literal: Thereupon he was made to turn his back and recite it—not one graph was wrong.
translations.idiomatic: He turned away and recited it without a single error.

sentences.zh: 觀人圍碁，粲為覆之，潟者不信，以帊蓋之，更以它局為之，不誤一道。

translations.literal: Watching others play weiqi, Can reset the game; the onlooker did not believe it, covered it with a cloth, and set up another board from memory—he did not miss one move.
translations.idiomatic: After watching a game of weiqi, he reproduced it under a cloth on a fresh board without a mistake.

sentences.zh: 年四十卒。

translations.literal: He died at forty sui.
translations.idiomatic: He died at forty.

sentences.zh: 魏志有傳。

translations.literal: The Wei Records has his biography.
translations.idiomatic: His life is recorded in the Sanguo zhi, Wei section.

sentences.zh: 種暠字景伯，河南洛陽人，仲山甫之後也。

translations.literal: Zhong Hao styled Jingbo was a man of Luoyang in Henan, a descendant of Zhong Shanfu.
translations.idiomatic: Zhong Hao of Luoyang traced his line to Zhong Shanfu.

sentences.zh: 父為定陶令，有財三千萬。

translations.literal: His father had been magistrate of Dingtao and possessed wealth of thirty million cash.
translations.idiomatic: His father died rich as magistrate of Dingtao with thirty million in cash.

sentences.zh: 父卒，暠悉以賑恤宗族及邑裡之貧者。

translations.literal: When his father died, Hao used all of it to relieve and aid the agnates and the poor of the town and village.
translations.idiomatic: Zhong Hao gave the entire fortune to his clan and the local poor.

sentences.zh: 其有進趣名利，皆不與交通。

translations.literal: Those who advanced toward profit and fame—he had no dealings with any of them.
translations.idiomatic: He shunned careerists chasing office and money.

sentences.zh: 始為縣門下史。

translations.literal: He began as a clerk under the county gate.
translations.idiomatic: He started as a gate clerk in the county yamen.

sentences.zh: 時河南尹田歆外甥王諶，名知人。

translations.literal: At that time the governor of Henan, Tian Xin, had a sister's son Wang Chen, famed for knowing men.
translations.idiomatic: Henan governor Tian Xin's nephew Wang Chen had a reputation for spotting talent.

sentences.zh: [一]歆謂之曰：「今當舉六孝廉，多得貴戚書命，不宜相違，欲自用一名士以報國家，爾助我求之。

translations.literal: [One] Xin said to him: 'Now I must recommend six filial and incorrupt men; I have mostly received letters and orders from honored kin—it is not fitting to disobey them; I wish to use one place myself for a scholar who will repay the state—you help me seek him.'
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: Tian Xin needed six nominees but noble pressure filled five slots; he begged Wang Chen to find one true scholar.

sentences.zh: 」明日，諶送客於大陽郭，遙見暠，異之。

translations.literal: The next day Chen saw off a guest at the outer wall of Dayang and from afar saw Hao and marveled at him.
translations.idiomatic: Next day Wang Chen spotted Zhong Hao outside Dayang and was struck by him.

sentences.zh: 還白歆曰：「為尹得孝廉矣，近洛陽門下史也。

translations.literal: Returning he reported to Xin: 'For the governor I have found a filial and incorrupt man—he is the clerk under the gate near Luoyang.'
translations.idiomatic: He reported: your sixth man is the Luoyang gate clerk.

sentences.zh: 」歆笑曰：

translations.literal: Xin laughed and said:
translations.idiomatic: Tian Xin laughed and said,

sentences.zh: 「當得山澤隱滯，*

translations.literal: 'I ought to obtain someone hidden and stuck in mountains and marshes,*
translations.idiomatic: I expected a recluse from hill and marsh,

translations.literal: (near)
translations.idiomatic: (near)

sentences.zh: **[乃]*洛陽吏邪？

translations.literal: **[nai]* a Luoyang clerk?'
translations.idiomatic: not some clerk from the capital?'

sentences.zh: 」諶曰：「山澤不必有異士，異士不必在山澤。

translations.literal: Chen said: 'Mountains and marshes need not hold unusual men; unusual men need not be in mountains and marshes.'
translations.idiomatic: Wang Chen replied that genius need not hide in the hills.

sentences.zh: 」歆即召暠於庭，辯詰職事。

translations.literal: Xin thereupon summoned Hao to the courtyard and debated and examined his duties.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Xin called Zhong Hao in and questioned him on administration.

sentences.zh: 暠辭對有序，歆甚知之，召署主簿，遂舉孝廉，辟太尉府，舉高第。

translations.literal: Hao replied in orderly sequence; Xin greatly understood him, summoned him to act as chief clerk, thereupon recommended him as filial and incorrupt, summoned him to the grand commandant's office, and recommended him with high rank.
translations.idiomatic: Zhong Hao passed every test; Tian Xin made him chief clerk, nominated him filial and incorrupt, and sent him to high office.

sentences.zh: 注[一]有知人之名也。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: He had a reputation for knowing men.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: Wang Chen was known as a judge of character.

sentences.zh: 順帝末，為侍御史。

translations.literal: At the end of Emperor Shun's reign he was attending secretary.
translations.idiomatic: Late in Shun's reign he became attending secretary.

sentences.zh: 時所遣八使光祿大夫杜喬﹑周舉等，多所糾奏，而大將軍梁冀及諸宦官互為請救，事皆被寑遏。

translations.literal: At that time the eight envoys—Grandees of Brilliant Achievement Du Qiao, Zhou Ju, and others—made many impeachments, but Grand General Liang Ji and the various eunuchs mutually pleaded for rescue, and the affairs were all suppressed and blocked.
translations.idiomatic: The Eight Exemplars' impeachments were buried when Liang Ji and the eunuchs traded favors.

sentences.zh: 暠自以職主刺舉，志案奸違，乃復劾諸為八使所舉蜀郡太守劉宣等罪惡章露，宜伏歐刀。

translations.literal: Hao himself, because his office was chiefly to impeach and investigate, intended to investigate wicked violations, and thereupon again impeached the several persons such as governor of Shu commandery Liu Xuan whom the eight envoys had cited—whose crimes and evils were manifest in their memorials and ought to face the executioner's blade.
translations.idiomatic: Zhong Hao re-opened cases against men like Liu Xuan of Shu whom the envoys had exposed, demanding capital punishment.

sentences.zh: 又奏請□四府條舉近臣父兄及知親為刺史﹑二千石尤殘穢不勝任者，免遣案罪。

translations.literal: He also memorialized requesting [lacuna] the four offices jointly to memorialize close ministers' fathers, elder brothers, and trusted kin who were governors or two-thousand-bushel men especially cruel, filthy, and unfit for office—to dismiss and send them for trial of crimes.
translations.idiomatic: He asked the four highest offices to purge corrupt governors related to the inner court.

sentences.zh: 帝乃從之。

translations.literal: The emperor thereupon followed it.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor agreed.

sentences.zh: 擢暠監太子於承光宮。

translations.literal: He promoted Hao to supervise the heir apparent in Chengguang Palace.
translations.idiomatic: Zhong Hao was made tutor to the crown prince in Chengguang Palace.

sentences.zh: 中常侍高梵從中單駕出迎太子，時太傅杜喬等疑不欲從，惶惑不知所為。

translations.literal: Palace Attendant Gao Fan drove out from within with a single team to welcome the heir; at that time Grand Tutor Du Qiao and others doubted and did not wish to comply, anxious and confused, not knowing what to do.
translations.idiomatic: When Gao Fan came with a lone carriage to fetch the prince, Du Qiao and the tutors hesitated.

sentences.zh: 暠乃手□當車，曰：「太子國之儲副，人命所繫。

translations.literal: Hao thereupon with his hand [lacuna] blocked the carriage, saying: 'The heir is the state's reserve deputy; human lives hang on him.
translations.idiomatic: Zhong Hao physically blocked the chariot: the crown prince carried the dynasty's fate.

sentences.zh: 今常侍來無詔信，何以知非奸邪？

translations.literal: Now the attendant has come with no edict or credentials—how do we know he is not treacherous?'
translations.idiomatic: No sealed edict accompanied the eunuch—how could they know it was not a trap?'

sentences.zh: 今日有死而已。

translations.literal: Today there is only death.'
translations.idiomatic: He would die before letting the prince go.'

sentences.zh: 」梵辭屈，不敢對，馳命奏之。

translations.literal: Fan's words were defeated; he dared not answer, galloped off orders and memorialized.
translations.idiomatic: Gao Fan, speechless, raced back to report.

sentences.zh: 詔報，太子乃得去。

translations.literal: An edict replied, and only then could the heir leave.
translations.idiomatic: An edict arrived; then the prince could depart.

sentences.zh: 喬退而歎息，愧暠臨事不惑。

translations.literal: Qiao withdrew and sighed, ashamed that Hao in facing affairs was not confused.
translations.idiomatic: Du Qiao withdrew, ashamed that Zhong Hao alone had kept his head.

sentences.zh: 帝亦嘉其持重，稱善者良久。

translations.literal: The emperor also praised his steadiness and for a long time spoke well of him.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor too praised his composure.

sentences.zh: 出為益州刺史。

translations.literal: He went out as governor of Yi province.
translations.idiomatic: He became governor of Yi province.

sentences.zh: 暠素慷慨，好立功立事。

translations.literal: Hao by nature was generous and ardent, fond of establishing merit and accomplishing deeds.
translations.idiomatic: Zhong Hao was bold and ambitious.

sentences.zh: 在職三年，宣恩遠夷，開曉殊俗，岷山雜落皆懷服漢德。

translations.literal: In office three years he spread grace to distant Yi, opened and enlightened alien customs, and the mixed settlements on Mount Min all cherished and submitted to Han virtue.
translations.idiomatic: In three years he won the tribes of Minshan to Han rule.

sentences.zh: 其白狼﹑盤木﹑唐菆﹑滘﹑僰諸國，[一]自前刺史朱輔卒後遂絕；

translations.literal: The states of Bailang, Panmu, Tangzou, Jiao, Bo, and others, [one] since the former governor Zhu Fu died had broken off;
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: Tribes such as Bailang had cut tribute since Zhu Fu died;

sentences.zh: 暠至，乃復舉種向化。

translations.literal: when Hao arrived, they again raised their tribes toward transformation.
translations.idiomatic: when Zhong Hao came, they renewed submission.

sentences.zh: 時永昌太守冶鑄黃金為文蛇，以獻梁冀，暠糾發逮捕，馳傳上言，而二府畏懦，不敢案之，冀由是銜怒於暠。

translations.literal: At that time the governor of Yongchang cast gold into a patterned serpent to present to Liang Ji; Hao impeached, exposed, and arrested him, sent post-haste upward to speak, but the two offices were timid and dared not try the case, whereupon Ji bore hatred in his heart toward Hao.
translations.idiomatic: He arrested Yongchang's governor for bribing Liang Ji with a golden snake; the high ministers quailed, and Liang Ji hated him for it.

sentences.zh: 會巴郡人服直聚黨數百人，自稱「天王」，[二]暠與太守應承討捕，不克，吏人多被傷害。

translations.literal: It happened that a man of Ba commandery, Fu Zhi, gathered a faction of several hundred who called themselves 'Heaven's King,' [two] Hao together with governor Ying Cheng attacked and seized them but could not overcome them; many clerks and people were harmed.
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: A cult leader Fu Zhi in Ba rose as 'Heaven's King'; Zhong Hao and Ying Cheng failed to suppress him and took casualties.

sentences.zh: 冀因此陷之，傳逮暠﹑承。

translations.literal: Ji by this trapped them, transmitting orders to seize Hao and Cheng.
translations.idiomatic: Liang Ji used the failure to impeach Zhong Hao and Ying Cheng.

sentences.zh: 太尉李固上疏救曰：「臣伏聞討捕所傷，本非暠﹑承之意，實由縣吏懼法畏罪，迫逐深苦，致此不詳。

translations.literal: Grand Commandant Li Gu submitted a memorial of rescue saying: 'Your servant has heard that the harm in the attack and seizure was originally not the intent of Hao and Cheng; truly it was because county clerks feared the law and dreaded crime, pressed pursuit in bitter depth, and brought about this ill-omened outcome.'
translations.idiomatic: Li Gu argued the defeat came from harsh local clerks, not the governors' orders.

sentences.zh: 比盜賊腢起，處處未絕。

translations.literal: Moreover bandits and robbers lately rise everywhere and are not yet cut off.
translations.idiomatic: Rebels were rising everywhere.

sentences.zh: 暠﹑承以首舉大奸，而相隨受罪，臣恐沮傷州縣糾發之意，更共飾匿，莫復盡心。

translations.literal: Hao and Cheng for first exposing great wickedness yet follow in receiving punishment; your servant fears this will discourage the intent of commanderies and counties to impeach and expose, and they will together gloss over and hide, never again exhausting their hearts.
translations.idiomatic: Punishing men who first exposed major crime would make officials cover up wrongdoing.

sentences.zh: 」[三] 梁太后省奏，乃赦暠﹑承罪，免官而已。

translations.literal: '[Three] Empress Dowager Liang examined the memorial and thereupon pardoned Hao and Cheng's crimes, dismissing from office only.'
translations.idiomatic: Note [3]: Empress Dowager Liang read Li Gu's plea and pardoned them, stripping only their posts.'

sentences.zh: 注[一]菆音側留反。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: Zou has the sound ce liu fan.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: The graph zou is read ce-liu.

sentences.zh: 注[二]「直」或作「宜」。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: 'Zhi' is sometimes written 'Yi.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [2]: Manuscript variant Yi for Zhi in the rebel's name.

sentences.zh: 注[三]言各飾偽辭，隱匿真狀也。

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: It means each glosses false words and hides the true condition.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [3]: They would invent excuses and conceal facts.

sentences.zh: 後涼州羌動，以暠為涼州刺史，甚得百姓歡心。

translations.literal: Later when the Qiang of Liangzhou stirred, Hao was made governor of Liangzhou and greatly won the joy of the common people.
translations.idiomatic: When Liangzhou's Qiang rebelled, Zhong Hao as governor won the people's love.

sentences.zh: 被征當遷，吏人詣闕請留之，太后歎曰：「未聞刺史得人心若是。

translations.literal: When he was summoned for promotion, clerks and people went to the palace gate begging to retain him; the empress dowager sighed: 'I have never heard of a governor winning hearts like this.'
translations.idiomatic: Officials petitioned to keep him; the empress dowager marveled.

sentences.zh: 」乃許之。

translations.literal: Thereupon she permitted it.
translations.idiomatic: She granted an extension.

sentences.zh: 暠復留一年，遷漢陽太守，戎夷男女送至漢陽界，暠與相揖謝，千里不得乘車。

translations.literal: Hao again stayed one year, was promoted governor of Hanyang; Yi and Di, men and women, escorted him to the border of Hanyang; Hao bowed to one another with thanks and for a thousand li did not ride in a carriage.
translations.idiomatic: After another year he moved to Hanyang; tribes escorted him on foot a thousand li as he walked beside them.

sentences.zh: 及到郡，化行羌胡，禁止侵掠。

translations.literal: When he reached the commandery, transformation proceeded among Qiang and Hu, and invasion and plunder were forbidden.
translations.idiomatic: In Hanyang he stopped raiding between Qiang and Hu.

sentences.zh: 遷使匈奴中郎將。

translations.literal: He was promoted envoy to the Xiongnu.
translations.idiomatic: He became envoy to the Xiongnu.

sentences.zh: 時遼東烏桓反叛，復轉遼東太守，烏桓望風率服，迎拜於界上。

translations.literal: At that time the Wuhuan of Liaodong rebelled; he was again transferred governor of Liaodong; the Wuhuan submitted at the wind's report and welcomed him bowing at the border.
translations.idiomatic: When Liaodong's Wuhuan rose, he was sent as governor; they surrendered at his approach and met him at the border.

sentences.zh: 坐事免歸。

translations.literal: He sat an affair and was dismissed and returned.
translations.idiomatic: He was later cashiered and sent home.

sentences.zh: 後司隸校尉舉暠賢良方正，不應。

translations.literal: Later the colonel director of retainers recommended Hao as worthy and upright; he did not respond.
translations.idiomatic: He declined nomination as worthy and upright.

sentences.zh: 征拜議郎，遷南郡太守，入為尚書。

translations.literal: He was summoned gentleman consultant, promoted governor of Nan commandery, and entered as master of writing.
translations.idiomatic: He was recalled as gentleman consultant, governor of Nan commandery, then master of writing.

sentences.zh: 會匈奴寇並涼二州，桓帝擢暠為度遼將軍。

translations.literal: It happened that the Xiongnu raided Bing and Liang provinces; Emperor Huan promoted Hao general who crosses the Liao.
translations.idiomatic: When the Xiongnu struck Bing and Liang, Huandi named him general of the Trans-Liao army.

sentences.zh: 暠到營所，先宣恩信，誘降諸胡，其有不服，然後加討。

translations.literal: When Hao reached camp he first spread grace and trust, luring submission from the various Hu; those who did not submit, afterward he punished.
translations.idiomatic: He offered amnesty first, then struck only the holdouts.

sentences.zh: 羌虜先時有生見獲質於郡縣者，悉遣還之。

translations.literal: Among Qiang captives who earlier had been taken alive as hostages in commanderies and counties, he sent them all back.
translations.idiomatic: He freed all Qiang held hostage in border counties.

sentences.zh: 誠心懷撫，信賞分明，由是羌胡﹑龜茲﹑莎車﹑烏孫等皆來順服。

translations.literal: With sincere heart he cherished and soothed them; trust and rewards were clear; whereupon Qiang, Hu, Qiuci, Shache, Wusun, and others all came to submit.
translations.idiomatic: Clear rewards and honest dealing brought Qiuci, Shache, Wusun, and others to allegiance.

sentences.zh: 暠乃去烽燧，除候望，[一]邊方晏然無警。

translations.literal: Hao thereupon removed beacon fires and cleared watch posts, [one] the border was tranquil without alarm.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: He dismantled beacons—the frontier lay quiet.

sentences.zh: 注[一]晝舉烽，夜燔燧。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: By day they raised beacon smoke, by night they burned signal fires.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: Day beacons, night bonfires on the wall.

sentences.zh: 解見光武紀。

translations.literal: The explanation is seen in the Annals of Emperor Guangwu.
translations.idiomatic: See Guangwu's annals for detail.

sentences.zh: 入為大司農。

translations.literal: He entered as grand minister of agriculture.
translations.idiomatic: He became grand minister of agriculture.

sentences.zh: 延熹四年，遷司徒。

translations.literal: In the fourth year of Yanxi he was promoted minister of education.
translations.idiomatic: In 161 CE he became minister of education.

sentences.zh: 推達名臣橋玄﹑皇甫規等，為稱職相。

translations.literal: He promoted and reached famous ministers such as Qiao Xuan and Huangfu Gui, becoming a partner who matched their posts.
translations.idiomatic: He lifted men like Qiao Xuan and Huangfu Gui to office worthy of them.

sentences.zh: 在位三年，年六十一薨。

translations.literal: In office three years he died at sixty-one sui.
translations.idiomatic: He died in office at sixty-one.

sentences.zh: 並﹑涼邊人鹹為發哀。

translations.literal: Border people of Bing and Liang all mourned for him.
translations.idiomatic: Bing and Liang frontier folk mourned him.

sentences.zh: 匈奴聞暠卒，舉國傷惜。

translations.literal: The Xiongnu heard Hao had died and the whole state grieved and pitied.
translations.idiomatic: The Xiongnu mourned him nationally.

sentences.zh: 單于每入朝賀，望見墳墓，輒哭泣祭祀。

translations.literal: Whenever the chanyu entered court to congratulate, seeing the tomb mound he always wept and sacrificed.
translations.idiomatic: Each chanyu who visited wept at his grave.

sentences.zh: 二子：岱，拂。

translations.literal: Two sons: Dai and Fu.
translations.idiomatic: His sons were Zhong Dai and Zhong Fu.

sentences.zh: 岱字公祖。

translations.literal: Dai styled Gongzu.
translations.idiomatic: Zhong Dai, styled Gongzu.

sentences.zh: 好學養志。

translations.literal: He loved learning and cultivated his intent.
translations.idiomatic: He loved books and cultivated his mind.

sentences.zh: 舉孝廉﹑茂才，辟公府，皆不就。

translations.literal: Recommended filial and incorrupt and flourishing talent, summoned to the ducal offices—he did not go to any.
translations.idiomatic: He declined every high summons.

sentences.zh: 公車特徵，病卒。

translations.literal: The public carriage specially summoned him; he died of illness.
translations.idiomatic: He died while awaiting a special summons to the capital.

sentences.zh: 初，岱與李固子燮同征議郎，燮聞岱卒，痛惜甚，乃上書求加禮於岱。

translations.literal: At first Dai had been summoned together with Li Gu's son Xie as gentlemen consultants; when Xie heard Dai had died he grieved extremely and thereupon submitted a memorial requesting added rites for Dai.
translations.idiomatic: Li Xie, son of Li Gu, petitioned for posthumous honors for Zhong Dai.

sentences.zh: 曰：「臣聞仁義興則道德昌，道德昌則政化明，政化明而萬姓寧。

translations.literal: It stated: 'Your servant has heard that when benevolence and righteousness rise, then power and virtue flourish; when power and virtue flourish, then government and transformation are clear; when government and transformation are clear, then the myriad surnames are tranquil.'
translations.idiomatic: He wrote that virtue orders the realm and calms the people.

sentences.zh: 伏見故處士種岱，淳和達理，耽悅詩書，富貴不能回其慮，萬物不能擾其心。

translations.literal: I humbly see the late recluse Zhong Dai, pure and harmonious, penetrating pattern, addicted to delight in the Classic of Poetry and Documents—wealth and honor could not turn his thoughts, the myriad things could not disturb his heart.
translations.idiomatic: He praised Dai's purity and love of the classics, unmoved by wealth or distraction.

sentences.zh: 稟命不永，奄然殂殞。

translations.literal: He received fate not long and suddenly perished.
translations.idiomatic: Dai died young.

sentences.zh: 若不盤桓難進，等輩皆已公卿矣。

translations.literal: If he had not lingered and been hard to advance, his peers would all already have been dukes and ministers.
translations.idiomatic: Had he sought office, his equals would be chief ministers.

sentences.zh: [一]昔先賢既沒，有加贈之典，[二]周禮盛德，有銘誄之文，[三]而岱生無印綬之榮，卒無官謚之號。

translations.literal: [One] In former times when worthy forebears perished, there was the norm of added posthumous gifts; [two] the Zhou li for great virtue has the text of inscription and dirge; [three] yet Dai in life had no glory of seal-ribbons, in death no title of office or posthumous name.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1–3]: Ancient worthies received posthumous honors and inscriptions; Dai had neither rank nor posthumous title in life or death.

sentences.zh: 雖未建忠效用，而為聖恩所拔，遐邇具瞻，宜有異賞。

translations.literal: Though he did not establish loyalty or offer effect, yet he was lifted by sage grace; far and near all looked on him—there ought to be a special reward.
translations.idiomatic: Li Xie asked for special recognition though Dai had held no office.

sentences.zh: 」朝廷竟不能從。

translations.literal: The court in the end could not follow.
translations.idiomatic: The court refused.

sentences.zh: 注[一]易屯卦曰：「盤桓，利居貞。」

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: The Changes, Hexagram Tun, states: 'Lingering, favorable to dwell in correctness.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: From the Yijing, Tun hexagram on hesitation.

sentences.zh: 注[二]春秋隱公五年，臧僖伯卒，隱公葬之加一等。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: In the fifth year of Duke Yin of Spring and Autumn, Zang Xibo died; Duke Yin buried him with one added grade.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [2]: Duke Yin buried Zang Xibo with extra ritual rank.

sentences.zh: 杜預曰：「加命服之一等。」

translations.literal: Du Yu states: 'Adding one grade of mandated dress.'
translations.idiomatic: Du Yu: one step up in funeral regalia.

sentences.zh: 注[三]周禮司勳曰：「凡有功者，銘書於王之太常。

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: The Zhou li, Director of Merits, states: 'All who have merit inscribe writing on the king's grand constancy.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [3]: Zhou li on inscribing merit on the king's banner.

sentences.zh: 」又曰「卿大夫之喪，賜謚誄」也。

translations.literal: It also states: 'For the funeral of ministers and grandees, grant posthumous title and dirge.'
translations.idiomatic: Zhou li also mandates posthumous names and dirges for ministers.

sentences.zh: 岱弟拂

translations.literal: Dai's younger brother Fu.
translations.idiomatic: Zhong Dai's younger brother Zhong Fu.

sentences.zh: 拂字穎伯。

translations.literal: Fu styled Yingbo.
translations.idiomatic: Zhong Fu, styled Yingbo.

sentences.zh: 初為司隸從事，拜宛令。

translations.literal: At first he was clerk to the colonel director of retainers, was appointed magistrate of Wan.
translations.idiomatic: He began as a clerk under the colonel director of retainers, then magistrate of Wan.

sentences.zh: 時南陽郡吏好因休沐，遊戲市裡，為百姓所患。

translations.literal: At that time clerks of Nan commandery liked on rest days to roam and play in the market and were a trouble to the common people.
translations.idiomatic: Nanyang yamen clerks abused their days off to terrorize the market.

sentences.zh: 拂出逢之，必下車公謁，以愧其心，自是莫敢出者。

translations.literal: When Fu went out and met them, he always descended from his carriage and bowed to them in public audience, thereby to shame their hearts; from this none dared go out.
translations.idiomatic: Zhong Fu publicly bowed to them to shame them into staying home.

sentences.zh: 政有能名，累遷光祿大夫。

translations.literal: His government had a reputation for ability; he was repeatedly promoted grandee of brilliant achievement.
translations.idiomatic: His rule was famed; he rose to grandee of brilliant achievement.

sentences.zh: 初平元年，代荀爽為司空。

translations.literal: In the first year of Chuping he replaced Xun Shuang as grand commandant.
translations.idiomatic: In 190 CE he succeeded Xun Shuang as grand commandant.

sentences.zh: 明年，以地震策免，復為太常。

translations.literal: The next year he was dismissed by edict because of earthquake, again becoming grand master of ceremonies.
translations.idiomatic: Earthquakes cost him the grand commandantship; he became grand master of ceremonies again.

sentences.zh: 李傕、郭汜之亂，長安城潰，百官多避兵沖。

translations.literal: In the turmoil of Li Jue and Guo Si, the walls of Chang'an collapsed; many hundred officials fled the clash of arms.
translations.idiomatic: When Li Jue and Guo Si stormed Chang'an, many officials fled.

sentences.zh: 拂揮□而出曰：「為國大臣，不能止戈除暴，致使凶賊兵刃向宮，去欲何之！

translations.literal: Fu waved [lacuna] and went out saying: 'As great ministers of the state, unable to stop spears and remove violence, we have caused villainous rebels to turn blades toward the palace—where do you think you are going!'
translations.idiomatic: Zhong Fu shouted that ministers who could not stop civil war had no right to flee the palace.

sentences.zh: 」遂戰而死。

translations.literal: Thereupon he fought and died.
translations.idiomatic: He stayed and died fighting.

sentences.zh: 子劭。

translations.literal: His son was Shao.
translations.idiomatic: His son was Zhong Shao.

sentences.zh: 拂子劭

translations.literal: Fu's son Shao
translations.idiomatic: Zhong Fu's son Zhong Shao

sentences.zh: 劭字申甫。

translations.literal: Shao styled Shenfu.
translations.idiomatic: Zhong Shao, styled Shenfu.

sentences.zh: 少知名。

translations.literal: In youth he was known for his name.
translations.idiomatic: He was famous while young.

sentences.zh: 中平末，為諫議大夫。

translations.literal: At the end of Zhongping he was remonstrance counselor.
translations.idiomatic: Late in Zhongping (189 CE) he became remonstrance counselor.

sentences.zh: 大將軍何進將誅宦官，召并州牧董卓，至澠池，而進意更狐疑，遣劭宣詔止之。

translations.literal: Grand General He Jin was about to execute the eunuchs and summoned Bingzhou shepherd Dong Zhuo; when Zhuo reached Mianchi, Jin's mind grew more fox-like in doubt and he dispatched Shao to proclaim an edict stopping him.
translations.idiomatic: He Jin wavered and sent Zhong Shao with an edict to halt Dong Zhuo at Mianchi.

sentences.zh: 卓不受，遂前至河南。

translations.literal: Zhuo did not accept and thereupon advanced to Henan.
translations.idiomatic: Dong Zhuo ignored the order and marched to Henan.

sentences.zh: 劭迎勞之，因譬令還軍。

translations.literal: Shao went to welcome and console him and thereby explained, ordering him to turn the army back.
translations.idiomatic: Zhong Shao met him and urged him to withdraw.

sentences.zh: 卓疑有變，使其軍士以兵脅劭。

translations.literal: Zhuo suspected there was a change and caused his soldiers to threaten Shao with weapons.
translations.idiomatic: Dong Zhuo had troops menace him.

sentences.zh: 劭怒，稱詔大呼叱之，軍士皆披，[一]遂前質責卓。

translations.literal: Shao was angry, proclaimed the edict and loudly shouted at them; the soldiers all scattered, [one] thereupon he advanced and confronted Zhuo with reproach.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: Zhong Shao roared out the imperial order; the soldiers broke; he rebuked Zhuo to his face.

sentences.zh: 卓辭屈，乃還軍夕陽亭。

translations.literal: Zhuo's words were defeated; thereupon he returned the army to Xiyang Pavilion.
translations.idiomatic: Dong Zhuo yielded and camped at Xiyang Pavilion.

translations.literal: [Two]
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: (marker)

sentences.zh: 注[一]披音芳靡反。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: Pi has the sound fang mi fan.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: Pi is read fang-mi (scatter).

sentences.zh: 注[二]夕陽亭在河南城西。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: Xiyang Pavilion is west of Henan city.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [2]: Xiyang ting lay west of Luoyang.

sentences.zh: 及進敗，獻帝即位，拜劭為侍中。

translations.literal: When Jin was defeated and Emperor Xian ascended, Shao was appointed palace attendant.
translations.idiomatic: After He Jin fell and Emperor Xian took the throne, Zhong Shao became palace attendant.

sentences.zh: 卓既□權，而惡劭強力，遂左轉議郎，出為益涼二州刺史。

translations.literal: Once Zhuo had [lacuna] power and hated Shao's forcefulness, thereupon he turned him left to gentleman consultant and sent him out as governor of Yi and Liang provinces.
translations.idiomatic: Dong Zhuo resented his nerve and demoted him to consultant, nominally governor of two provinces.

sentences.zh: 會父拂戰死，竟不之職。

translations.literal: It happened that his father Fu died in battle; in the end he did not go to office.
translations.idiomatic: His father Zhong Fu died fighting Li Jue; he never took up the post.

sentences.zh: 服終，征為少府、大鴻臚，皆辭不受。

translations.literal: When mourning ended, he was summoned as privy treasurer and grand herald—he all declined and did not accept.
translations.idiomatic: After mourning he refused summons as privy treasurer and grand herald.

sentences.zh: 曰：「昔我先父以身徇國，吾為臣子，不能除殘復怨，何面目朝覲明主哉！

translations.literal: He stated: 'In former times my late father gave his body for the state; I as son and minister could not remove the remnant and avenge the wrong—what face have I to attend audience on an enlightened lord!'
translations.idiomatic: He said he could not face the throne until he avenged his father.

sentences.zh: 」遂與馬騰、韓遂及左中郎劉范、諫議大夫馬宇共攻李傕、郭汜，以報其仇。

translations.literal: Thereupon together with Ma Teng, Han Sui, and Palace Gentleman of the Left Liu Fan and Remonstrance Counselor Ma Yu he jointly attacked Li Jue and Guo Si to repay their enmity.
translations.idiomatic: He joined Ma Teng, Han Sui, Liu Fan, and Ma Yu against Li Jue and Guo Si.

sentences.zh: 與汜戰於長平觀下，[一]軍敗，劭等皆死。

translations.literal: They fought Si below Changping Slope, [one] the army was defeated and Shao and the others all died.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: They lost the battle below Changping; Zhong Shao died.

sentences.zh: 勝遂還涼州。

translations.literal: Han Sui thereupon returned to Liangzhou.
translations.idiomatic: Han Sui withdrew to Liangzhou.

sentences.zh: 注[一]長平，阪名也。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: Changping is the name of a slope.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: Changping was a hill slope.

sentences.zh: 有觀，在長安西十五里也。

translations.literal: There is a lookout tower; it is fifteen li west of Chang'an.
translations.idiomatic: A watchtower stood fifteen li west of Chang'an.

sentences.zh: 陳球字伯真，下邳淮浦人也。

translations.literal: Chen Qiu styled Bozhen was a man of Huai'pu in Xiapi.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Qiu of Huai'pu, Xiapi, styled Bozhen.

sentences.zh: 歷世著名。

translations.literal: For generations they were famous.
translations.idiomatic: His family had been eminent for generations.

sentences.zh: [一]父亹，廣漢太守。

translations.literal: [One] His father Wei was governor of Guanghan.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: His father Chen Wei governed Guanghan.

sentences.zh: [二]球少涉儒學，善律令。

translations.literal: [Two] Qiu in youth crossed into Confucian learning and was good at statutes and ordinances.
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: Chen Qiu studied the classics and law.

sentences.zh: 陽嘉中，舉孝廉，稍遷繁陽令。

translations.literal: In the Yangjia era he was recommended filial and incorrupt and gradually was promoted magistrate of Fanyang.
translations.idiomatic: During Yangjia he rose to magistrate of Fanyang in Wei commandery.

sentences.zh: [三]時魏郡太守諷縣求納貨賄，球不與之，太守怒而撾督郵，欲令逐球。

translations.literal: [Three] At that time the governor of Wei commandery hinted to the county to seek to receive goods and bribes; Qiu did not give them; the governor was angry and beat the director of retainers, wishing to make him expel Qiu.
translations.idiomatic: Note [3]: The Wei governor demanded bribes; Chen Qiu refused; the governor beat the director of retainers to force Chen Qiu out.

sentences.zh: [四]督郵不肯，曰：「魏郡十五城，獨繁陽有異政，今受命逐之，將致議於天下矣。

translations.literal: [Four] The director of retainers would not consent, saying: 'Wei commandery has fifteen cities; alone Fanyang has unusual government; now receiving orders to expel him will bring censure from all under Heaven.'
translations.idiomatic: Note [4]: The director of retainers refused: only Fanyang was well ruled; expelling Chen Qiu would shame the governor empire-wide.

sentences.zh: 」太守乃止。

translations.literal: The governor thereupon stopped.
translations.idiomatic: The governor relented.

sentences.zh: 注[一]謝承書曰：「祖父屯，有令名。」

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: Xie Cheng's book states: 'The grandfather Tun had a fine reputation.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: Xie Cheng notes Chen Tun's good name.

sentences.zh: 注[二]亹音尾。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: Wei has the sound wei.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [2]: Wei is read wei.

sentences.zh: 注[三]繁陽，魏郡縣。

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: Fanyang is a county of Wei commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [3]: Fanyang lay in Wei commandery.

sentences.zh: 注[四]撾，擊也。

translations.literal: Commentary [four]: Zhua means strike.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [4]: Zhua means 'to beat.'

sentences.zh: 復辟公府，舉高第，拜侍御史。

translations.literal: Again he was summoned to the ducal offices, recommended with high rank, and appointed attending secretary.
translations.idiomatic: He was recalled to high rank as attending secretary.

sentences.zh: 是時，桂陽黠賊李研等腢聚寇鈔，陸梁荊部，州郡懦弱，不能禁，太尉楊秉表球為零陵太守。

translations.literal: At that time the cunning bandits of Guiyang, Li Yan and others, gathered in raiding bands and trampled the Jing region; commanderies and provinces were weak and could not prohibit them; Grand Commandant Yang Bing memorialized Qiu as governor of Lingling.
translations.idiomatic: When Guiyang bandits overran Jing, Yang Bing named Chen Qiu governor of Lingling.

sentences.zh: 球到，設方略，開月閒，賊虜消散。

translations.literal: When Qiu arrived he set stratagems; within a month the bandit captives dispersed.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Qiu broke the gangs within a month.

sentences.zh: 而州兵朱蓋等反，與桂陽賊胡蘭數萬人轉攻零陵。

translations.literal: But provincial troops under Zhu Gai and others rebelled and together with Guiyang bandit Hu Lan with several tens of thousands turned to attack Lingling.
translations.idiomatic: Then Zhu Gai's mutinous troops joined Hu Lan's tens of thousands against Lingling.

sentences.zh: 零陵下濕，編木為城，不可守備，郡中惶恐。

translations.literal: Lingling was low and damp; they wove wood for walls—it could not be defended; the commandery was terrified.
translations.idiomatic: Lingling's soggy soil and wicker palisade could not hold.

sentences.zh: 掾史白遣家避難，球怒曰：「太守分國虎符，受任一邦，[一]豈顧妻孥而沮國威重乎？

translations.literal: Clerks reported suggesting sending families away to escape disaster; Qiu angrily said: 'The governor divides the state's tiger tally and receives charge of one region—[one] could he look to wife and children and damp the state's awesome weight?'
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: When aides urged evacuating families, Chen Qiu swore he held the tiger tally and would not flee with kin.

sentences.zh: 復言者斬！

translations.literal: Whoever speaks again will be beheaded!'
translations.idiomatic: He threatened death for anyone who repeated the counsel.'

sentences.zh: 」乃悉內吏人老弱，與共城守，弦大木為弓，羽矛為矢，引機發之，遠射千余步，多所殺傷。

translations.literal: Thereupon he brought in all the clerks and people, old and weak, and with them jointly defended the city; he strung great wood for bows and feathered spears for arrows, drew triggers to release them, shooting more than a thousand paces and killing and wounding many.
translations.idiomatic: He rallied everyone, built giant crossbows from timbers and spear-shafts as bolts, and slaughtered the enemy at long range.

sentences.zh: 賊復激流灌城，球輒於內因地埶反決水淹賊。

translations.literal: The bandits again channeled rushing water to flood the city; Qiu then on the inside according to terrain reversed the flow to drown the bandits.
translations.idiomatic: When rebels tried to flood the city, he counter-flooded them.

sentences.zh: 相拒十餘日，不能下。

translations.literal: They opposed one another more than ten days and could not take it.
translations.idiomatic: The siege failed for ten days.

sentences.zh: 會中郎將度尚將救兵至，球募士卒，與尚共破斬朱蓋等。

translations.literal: It happened that General of the Gentlemen of the Household Shang Du arrived with relief troops; Qiu recruited soldiers and together with Shang jointly defeated and beheaded Zhu Gai and others.
translations.idiomatic: Du Shang arrived with relief; Chen Qiu joined him to kill Zhu Gai.

sentences.zh: 賜錢五十萬，拜子一人為郎。

translations.literal: He was granted five hundred thousand cash and one son was appointed gentleman.
translations.idiomatic: The court gave half a million cash and a son a gentleman post.

sentences.zh: 遷魏郡太守。

translations.literal: He was promoted governor of Wei commandery.
translations.idiomatic: He became governor of Wei commandery.

sentences.zh: 注[一]文帝初與郡守分銅虎符。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: At the beginning of Emperor Wen's reign he divided bronze tiger tallies with commandery governors.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: Wendi split tiger tallies with governors.

sentences.zh: 征拜將作大匠，作桓帝陵園，所省巨萬以上。

translations.literal: He was summoned master of works and built Emperor Huan's mausoleum park, saving more than ten thousand in great sums.
translations.idiomatic: As master of works he built Huandi's tomb and saved immense sums.

sentences.zh: 遷南陽太守，以糾舉豪右，為執家所謗，征詣廷尉抵罪。

translations.literal: He was promoted governor of Nanyang; because he impeached and investigated great families on the right, he was slandered by powerful families and summoned to the minister of justice to answer for crimes.
translations.idiomatic: In Nanyang he attacked magnates; they framed him and he faced the minister of justice.

sentences.zh: 會赦，歸家。

translations.literal: It happened that there was an amnesty; he returned home.
translations.idiomatic: An amnesty sent him home.

sentences.zh: [三]詔公卿大會朝堂，令中常侍趙忠監議。

translations.literal: [Three] An edict ordered the dukes to assemble in the court hall and caused Palace Attendant Zhao Zhong to supervise the deliberation.
translations.idiomatic: Note [3]: The emperor convened the high ministers under eunuch Zhao Zhong's eye to debate burial rites.

sentences.zh: 太尉李鹹時病，乃扶輿而起，搗椒自隨，謂妻子曰：「若皇太后不得配食桓帝，吾不生還矣。

translations.literal: Grand Commandant Li Xian at that time was ill; thereupon he supported himself in a litter and rose, pounded pepper to bring with him, and said to wife and children: 'If the empress dowager may not share sacrifice with Emperor Huan, I will not return alive.'
translations.idiomatic: Li Xian, ill, had pepper carried to the debate, vowing to die if Empress Dowager Dou were denied joint burial with Huandi.

sentences.zh: 」既議，坐者數百人，各瞻望中官，良久莫肯先言。

translations.literal: When the deliberation began, those seated were several hundred persons; each looked toward the inner officials; for a long time none was willing to speak first.
translations.idiomatic: Hundreds of officials sat silent, watching the eunuchs.

sentences.zh: 趙忠曰：「議當時定。

translations.literal: Zhao Zhong said: 'The deliberation ought to be settled at once.'
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Zhong demanded a verdict.

sentences.zh: 」怪公卿以下各相顧望。

translations.literal: He wondered that from the dukes downward each looked at one another.
translations.idiomatic: He sneered at their mutual glances.

sentences.zh: 球曰：「皇太后以盛德良家，母臨天下，宜配先帝，是無所疑。

translations.literal: Qiu said: 'The empress dowager, with abundant virtue from a good family, was mother to All under Heaven; she ought to share sacrifice with the former emperor—there is no doubt in this.'
translations.idiomatic: Chen Qiu said Empress Dowager Dou must share Huandi's tomb.

sentences.zh: 」忠笑而言曰：「陳廷尉宜便操筆。

translations.literal: Zhong laughed and spoke saying: 'Minister of Justice Chen ought at once to take up the brush.'
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Zhong told Chen Qiu to draft the memorial.

sentences.zh: 」球即下議曰：「皇太后自在椒房，有聰明母儀之德。

translations.literal: Qiu thereupon descended with the deliberation saying: 'The empress dowager from the time she was in the Pepper Chamber had the virtue of a wise and motherly model.'
translations.idiomatic: Chen Qiu's text praised her virtue in the harem.

sentences.zh: 遭時不造，援立聖明，承繼宗廟，功烈至重。

translations.literal: She encountered untimely fate, aided in establishing the sage clarity, continued the ancestral temple—her merit and blazing deed are supremely weighty.
translations.idiomatic: She had enthroned the emperor and preserved the line.

sentences.zh: 先帝晏駕，因遇大獄，遷居空宮，不幸早世，家雖獲罪，事非太后。

translations.literal: The former emperor expired on the coach; because she met great imprisonment she was moved to dwell in an empty palace; unluckily she died early; though the family received crime, the affair was not the empress dowager's.
translations.idiomatic: He recounted her wrongful imprisonment and early death apart from her family's guilt.

sentences.zh: 今若別葬，誠失天下之望。

translations.literal: Now if she is separately buried, truly we lose the hope of All under Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: Separate burial would disappoint the realm.

sentences.zh: 且馮貴人頤墓被發，骸骨暴露，與賊並屍，魂靈污染，[四]且無功於國，何宜上配至尊？

translations.literal: Moreover the Honored Lady Feng's mausoleum was dug up, bones exposed, lying with bandits in one corpse—the spirit soul defiled, [four] moreover without merit to the state—how is she fit to ascend and match the supreme?'
translations.idiomatic: Note [4]: Lady Feng's tomb had been robbed; she could not rank above Dou.'

sentences.zh: 」忠省球議，作色俛仰，蚩球曰：「陳廷尉建此議甚健！

translations.literal: Zhong examined Qiu's deliberation, changed color looking up and down, and sneered at Qiu saying: 'Minister of Justice Chen's establishing this deliberation is very bold!'
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Zhong mocked Chen Qiu's bold memorial.

sentences.zh: 」球曰：「陳、竇既冤，皇太后無故幽閉，臣常痛心，天下憤歎。

translations.literal: Qiu said: 'Chen and Dou were already wronged; the empress dowager without cause was shut in seclusion—your servant always grieved in heart and All under Heaven sighed in anger.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Qiu retorted that Chen Fan and Dou Wu had been murdered and Dou imprisoned unjustly.

sentences.zh: 今日言之，退而受罪，宿昔之願。

translations.literal: Today speaking of it, withdrawing afterward to receive punishment—that is a wish of long standing.'
translations.idiomatic: He welcomed punishment for speaking truth.

sentences.zh: 」公卿以下，皆從球議。

translations.literal: From the dukes downward, all followed Qiu's deliberation.
translations.idiomatic: The assembly backed Chen Qiu.

sentences.zh: 李鹹始不敢先發，見球辭正，然*[後]*大言曰：「臣本謂宜爾，誠與臣意合。

translations.literal: Li Xian at first did not dare speak first; seeing Qiu's words correct, then*[after]* he spoke loudly: 'Your servant originally thought it ought to be so; truly it matches your servant's intent.'
translations.idiomatic: Li Xian, who had hung back, then claimed he had always agreed.

sentences.zh: 」會者皆為之愧。

translations.literal: Those at the meeting all felt shame for him.
translations.idiomatic: The court sneered at Li Xian's cowardice.

sentences.zh: 曹節、王甫復爭，以為梁後家犯惡逆，別葬懿陵，武帝黜廢□後，而以李夫人配食。

translations.literal: Cao Jie and Wang Fu again disputed, holding that Empress Liang's family had committed foul treason and was separately buried at Yiling, and Emperor Wu demoted and abolished [lacuna] the empress and made Lady Li share sacrifice.
translations.idiomatic: The eunuchs cited Liang empress's separate burial and Wudi's demotion of Wei zi [sic] in favor of Lady Li.

sentences.zh: [五]今竇氏罪深，豈得合葬先帝乎？

translations.literal: [Five] Now the Dou clan's crime is deep—how can they obtain joint burial with the former emperor?'
translations.idiomatic: Note [5]: They asked how Dou could share Huandi's tomb given the clan's guilt.'

sentences.zh: 李鹹乃詣闕上疏曰：「臣伏惟章德竇後虐害恭懷，安思閻後家犯惡逆，而和帝無異葬之議，順朝無貶降之文。

translations.literal: Li Xian thereupon went to the palace gate and submitted a memorial saying: 'Your servant humbly considers that Empress Zhangde Dou cruelly harmed Gonghuai, that Empress An's Yan family committed foul treason—yet He had no discussion of different burial, and Shun's court had no text of demotion.'
translations.idiomatic: Li Xian countered with Zhangde and Yan empresses who were not denied proper burial despite family crimes.

sentences.zh: 至於□後，孝武皇帝身所廢□，不可以為比。

translations.literal: As for [lacuna] the empress, Emperor Xiaowu personally demoted [lacuna]—she cannot be taken as comparison.
translations.idiomatic: Wudi's deposed empress was not parallel to Dou.

sentences.zh: 今長樂太后尊號在身，親嘗稱制，坤育天下，[六]且援立聖明，光隆皇祚。

translations.literal: Now the Changlo empress dowager's honored title is on her person; she personally once held the regency and in earth's mode nurtured All under Heaven, [six] moreover aided in establishing sage clarity and brightly enlarged the imperial succession.
translations.idiomatic: Note [6]: Dou had ruled as regent and enthroned the emperor.

sentences.zh: 太后以陛下為子，陛下豈得不以太后為母？

translations.literal: The empress dowager takes Your Majesty as son—can Your Majesty not take the empress dowager as mother?'
translations.idiomatic: She was his mother by rite.'

sentences.zh: 子無黜母，臣無貶君，宜合葬宣陵，一如舊制。

translations.literal: A son does not dismiss a mother; a minister does not demote a lord—it is fitting to bury jointly at Xuanling according to the old system.'
translations.idiomatic: He urged joint burial at Xuanling per precedent.

sentences.zh: 」帝省奏，謂曹節等曰：「竇氏雖為不道，而太后有德於朕，不宜降黜。

translations.literal: The emperor examined the memorial and said to Cao Jie and others: 'Though the Dou clan behaved contrary to the Way, the empress dowager had virtue toward me—it is not fitting to demote and dismiss her.'
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Xian sided with Chen Qiu: Dou had been kind to him.

sentences.zh: 」節等無復言，於是議者乃定。

translations.literal: Jie and others had no further words; thereupon those deliberating were settled.
translations.idiomatic: The eunuchs fell silent; the decision was fixed.

sentences.zh: 鹹字符貞，汝南人。

translations.literal: Xian styled Zhenzhen, a man of Runan.
translations.idiomatic: Li Xian of Runan, styled Zhenzhen.

sentences.zh: 累經州郡，以廉干知名；

translations.literal: He passed through many commanderies and provinces and was known for purity and capability;
translations.idiomatic: He had served in many posts as an honest, capable official;

sentences.zh: 在朝清忠，權幸憚之。

translations.literal: in court he was pure and loyal; the powerful and favored feared him.
translations.idiomatic: at court his integrity intimidated favorites.

sentences.zh: 注[一]太后父竇武與陳蕃謀誅宦官，反為中常侍曹節矯詔殺武、蕃，遷太后焉。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: The empress dowager's father Dou Wu with Chen Fan plotted to execute the eunuchs; in turn Palace Attendant Cao Jie forged an edict killing Wu and Fan and moving the empress dowager.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: On Dou Wu, Chen Fan, and Cao Jie's coup.

sentences.zh: 注[二]大雅抑詩也。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: It is a verse from the Greater Odes, 'Yi.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [2]: From the Greater Odes, 'Yi.'

sentences.zh: 注[三]祔謂新死之主祔於先死者之廟，婦祔於其夫，所祔之□妾祔於妾祖姑也。

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: Fu means the newly dead lord being fu to the temple of the earlier dead; the wife fu to her husband; the fu'd [lacuna] concubine fu to her husband's concubine great-aunt.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [3]: Zhou li ritual of fu—ancestral pairing—lacuna in text.

sentences.zh: 注[四]段熲為河南尹，坐盜發馮貴人頤，左遷諫議大夫。

translations.literal: Commentary [four]: Duan Jiong was governor of Henan; because tombs of the Honored Lady Feng were robbed, he was demoted left to remonstrance counselor.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [4]: Duan Jiong was demoted over the Feng tomb robbery.

sentences.zh: 注[五]戾太子□皇后共太子斬江充，自殺。

translations.literal: Commentary [five]: Heir Li [lacuna] the empress and the heir together beheaded Jiang Chong and killed themselves.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [5]: Crown Prince Li and Wei zi [context: Wudi's heir]—lacuna in manuscript.

sentences.zh: 武帝崩，霍光緣上雅意，以李夫人配食也。

translations.literal: When Emperor Wu died, Huo Guang followed the late emperor's refined intent and made Lady Li share sacrifice.
translations.idiomatic: Huo Guang paired Lady Li with Wudi per the emperor's wish.

sentences.zh: 注[六]周易曰：「坤為母。」

translations.literal: Commentary [six]: The Zhou Changes states: 'Kun is mother.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [6]: Yijing: Kun represents the mother.

sentences.zh: 六年，遷球司空，以地震免。

translations.literal: In the sixth year he promoted Qiu to grand commandant and dismissed him because of earthquake.
translations.idiomatic: In 179 CE Chen Qiu became grand commandant but lost the post to an earthquake omen.

sentences.zh: 拜光祿大夫，復為廷尉、太常。

translations.literal: He was appointed grandee of brilliant achievement and again was minister of justice and grand master of ceremonies.
translations.idiomatic: He returned as grandee of brilliant achievement, minister of justice, and grand master of ceremonies.

sentences.zh: 光和元年，遷太尉，數月，以日食免。

translations.literal: In the first year of Guanghe he was promoted grand commandant; within several months he was dismissed because of solar eclipse.
translations.idiomatic: In 178 CE he was grand commandant briefly, then cashiered after an eclipse.

sentences.zh: 復拜光祿大夫。

translations.literal: Again he was appointed grandee of brilliant achievement.
translations.idiomatic: He was again named grandee of brilliant achievement.

sentences.zh: 明年，為永樂少府，[一]乃潛與司徒河閒劉合謀誅宦官。

translations.literal: The next year he became privy treasurer of Yongle, [one] and secretly with Minister of Education Liu He of Hejian plotted to execute the eunuchs.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: He became privy treasurer to Empress Dowager Yongle and conspired with Liu He against the eunuchs.

sentences.zh: 注[一]桓帝母孝崇皇后宮曰永樂，置太僕、太府。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: Emperor Huan's mother Empress Xiaochong's palace was called Yongle; grand coachman and grand provisioner were established.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: Yongle was Huandi's mother's palace establishment.

sentences.zh: 初，合兄侍中儵，與大將軍竇武同謀俱死，故合與球相結。

translations.literal: At first He's elder brother, Palace Attendant Liu You, had plotted together with Grand General Dou Wu and both died; therefore He and Qiu formed a bond.
translations.idiomatic: Liu He's brother had died with Dou Wu, so Liu He and Chen Qiu allied.

sentences.zh: 事未及發，球復以書勸合曰：「公出自宗室，位登台鼎，天下瞻望，社稷鎮□，豈得雷同容容無違而已？

translations.literal: Before the affair had issued, Qiu again with a letter urged He saying: 'You come from the imperial clan; your position mounts the tripods—All under Heaven looks up; the altars of soil and grain depend on you [lacuna]—how can you echo others in easy tolerance and nothing beyond compliance?'
translations.idiomatic: Chen Qiu urged Liu He not to sit idle while the realm watched.

sentences.zh: 今曹節等放縱為害，而久在左右，又公兄侍中受害節等，永樂太后所親知也。

translations.literal: Now Cao Jie and others run riot doing harm and have long been at the ruler's side; moreover your elder brother the palace attendant was harmed by Jie and others—the Yongle empress dowager knows it intimately.
translations.idiomatic: He reminded Liu He that Cao Jie had killed his brother and was known to the empress dowager.

sentences.zh: 今可表徙□尉陽球為司隸校尉，以次收節等誅之。

translations.literal: Now you can memorialize to move [lacuna] Captain Yang Qiu to be colonel director of retainers, and in sequence seize Jie and others and execute them.
translations.idiomatic: He proposed promoting Yang Qiu to colonel director of retainers to arrest Cao Jie—lacuna in one graph.

sentences.zh: 政出聖主，天下太平，可翹足而待也。

translations.literal: Government will issue from the sage lord; All under Heaven will be at peace—you may await it on tiptoe.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Qiu's letter promised that once the plot succeeded, good government would return and peace would follow quickly.

sentences.zh: 」又尚書劉納以正直忤宦官，出為步兵校尉，亦深勸於合。

translations.literal: Moreover Master of Writing Liu Na, because of his straightness, had offended the eunuchs and gone out as colonel of foot soldiers; he also deeply urged He.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Na, demoted for crossing the eunuchs, also pressed Liu He to act.

sentences.zh: 合曰：「凶豎多耳目，恐事未會，先受其禍。

translations.literal: He said: 'The vicious eunuchs have many eyes and ears; I fear before the affair convenes we will first receive their disaster.'
translations.idiomatic: Liu He feared leaks and preemptive strikes.

sentences.zh: 」納曰：「公為國棟樑，傾危不持，焉用彼相邪？

translations.literal: Na said: 'You are the state's pillar and beam—when it leans in peril and you do not support it, what use is that beam?'
translations.idiomatic: Liu Na quoted the Analects: a minister must shore up a tottering state.

sentences.zh: 」[一]合許諾，亦結謀陽球。

translations.literal: [One] He consented and also formed a plot with Yang Qiu.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: Liu He agreed and brought in Yang Qiu.

sentences.zh: 注[一]論語孔子之辭也。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: It is a phrase from Confucius in the Analects.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: The 'pillar' line comes from the Analects.

sentences.zh: 球小妻，程璜之女，璜用事宮中，所謂程大人也。

translations.literal: Qiu's concubine was the daughter of Cheng Huang; Huang held power in the palace—the so-called Lord Cheng.
translations.idiomatic: Yang Qiu's junior wife was eunuch Cheng Huang's daughter; Cheng Huang was influential at court.

sentences.zh: 節等頗得聞知，乃重賂於璜，且脅之。

translations.literal: Jie and others gradually learned of it and thereupon heavily bribed Huang and moreover threatened him.
translations.idiomatic: Cao Jie's faction bribed and threatened Cheng Huang.

sentences.zh: 璜懼迫，以球謀告節，節因共白帝曰：「合等常與藩國交通，有惡意。

translations.literal: Huang, pressed and afraid, reported Qiu's plot to Jie; Jie therefore together memorialized to the emperor saying: 'He and others constantly communicate with princely kingdoms and harbor evil intent.'
translations.idiomatic: Cheng Huang betrayed the plot; Cao Jie memorialized that Liu He was conspiring with the princes.

sentences.zh: 數稱永樂聲埶，受取狼籍。

translations.literal: They repeatedly praised the Yongle faction's power and influence and said their taking and receiving was in disorder.
translations.idiomatic: They accused the Yongle circle of corruption and swagger.

sentences.zh: 步兵校尉劉納及永樂少府陳球、□尉陽球交通書疏，謀議不軌。

translations.literal: Colonel of Foot Liu Na and Privy Treasurer of Yongle Chen Qiu and [lacuna] Captain Yang Qiu exchanged letters and plotted unorthodox courses.
translations.idiomatic: They framed Liu Na, Chen Qiu, and Yang Qiu for treasonous correspondence—lacuna in one office title.

sentences.zh: 」帝大怒，策免合，合與球及劉納、陽球皆下獄死。

translations.literal: The emperor was greatly angry, dismissed He by edict, and He together with Qiu and Liu Na and Yang Qiu were all thrown into prison and died.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor arrested Liu He, Chen Qiu, Liu Na, and Yang Qiu; all died in prison.

sentences.zh: 球時年六十二。

translations.literal: Qiu at that time was sixty-two sui.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Qiu was sixty-two.

sentences.zh: 子瑀，吳郡太守；

translations.literal: His son was Yu, governor of Wu commandery;
translations.idiomatic: His son Chen Yu governed Wu;

sentences.zh: 瑀弟琮，汝陰太守；

translations.literal: Yu's younger brother Cong was governor of Ruyin;
translations.idiomatic: Chen Cong governed Ruyin;

sentences.zh: 弟子珪，沛相；

translations.literal: his nephew Gui was chancellor of Pei;
translations.idiomatic: his nephew Chen Gui was chancellor of Pei;

sentences.zh: 珪子登，廣陵太守：並知名。

translations.literal: Gui's son Deng was governor of Guangling: together they were known for their names.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Gui's son Chen Deng governed Guangling—all were famous.

translations.literal: [One]
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: (marker introducing Xu Si anecdote)

sentences.zh: 汜曰：「昔遭亂過下邳，見元龍無客主之意，不相與語，自上大默臥，使客臥下默。

translations.literal: Si said: 'In former times passing through chaos I visited Xiapi; I saw Yuanlong without the intent of host and guest; he would not speak with me; he himself went to sleep on the great couch above in silence, making the guest sleep on the lower couch in silence.'
translations.idiomatic: Xu Si told Liu Bei that Chen Deng (Yuanlong) had snubbed him, taking the upper bed while leaving Xu on the lower.

sentences.zh: 」備曰：「君有國士之名。

translations.literal: Bei said: 'You have the reputation of an outstanding scholar of the state.'
translations.idiomatic: Liu Bei began: 'You enjoy a national reputation.'

sentences.zh: 今天下大亂，帝王失所，君須憂國忘家，有救世之意。

translations.literal: Now All under Heaven is in great chaos; the emperor has lost his place; you ought to worry for the state and forget your family, having the intent to save the age.'
translations.idiomatic: He said in such times Xu Si should think of saving the realm, not comfort.

sentences.zh: 乃求田問捨，言無可采，是元龍所諱也，何緣當與君語？

translations.literal: Yet you seek fields and ask about houses—your words are not worth gathering—this is what Yuanlong disdained; on what account ought he to have spoken with you?'
translations.idiomatic: Seeking land deals was why Chen Deng scorned Xu Si.

sentences.zh: 如我自臥百尺樓上，臥君於地下，何但上下默之閒哉！

translations.literal: If it were me, I would myself sleep on a hundred-foot tower and make you sleep underground—how would it be only the space between upper and lower couches!'
translations.idiomatic: Liu Bei said he would have put Xu Si far below, not merely on a lower bed.

sentences.zh: 」表大笑也。

translations.literal: Biao laughed greatly.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Biao roared with laughter.

sentences.zh: 贊曰：安儲遭譖，張卿有請。

translations.literal: The encomium states: Securing the heir, meeting slander—Minister Zhang had his plea.
translations.idiomatic: The encomium praises Zhang Gang for guarding the crown prince.

sentences.zh: [一]龔糾便佞，以直為眚。

translations.literal: [One] Gong impeached flatterers; taking straightness became fault.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: Wang Gong attacked sycophants and paid for his honesty.

sentences.zh: [二]二子過正，埋車堙井。

translations.literal: [Two] The two sons exceeded the mean—burying the cart, filling the well.
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: Zhang Gang and Wang Chang went to extremes—buried wheels, filled wells (figurative harshness).

sentences.zh: [三]種公自微，臨官以威。

translations.literal: [Three] Lord Zhong rose from the lowly; facing office he used awe.
translations.idiomatic: Note [3]: Zhong Hao rose from humble clerk to commanding awe.

sentences.zh: 陳球專議，桓思同歸。

translations.literal: Chen Qiu's sole memorial; Empress Huan's thought returned with him.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Qiu's lone memorial won Empress Dou joint burial with Huandi.

sentences.zh: 注[一]張□為廷尉，故曰卿。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: Zhang [lacuna] was minister of justice; therefore he is called minister.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: Zhang Hao as minister of justice is called qing.

sentences.zh: 注[二]眚，過也。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: Sheng means fault.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [2]: Sheng means 'fault.'

sentences.zh: 注[三]張綱埋輪，王*

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: Zhang Gang buried wheels; Wang*
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [3]: Zhang Gang buried his wheels; Wang

translations.literal: (Gong)
translations.idiomatic: (Gong)

translations.literal: **[Chang]* filled wells.
translations.idiomatic: Chang) is said to have filled wells—hyperbolic severity.

sentences.zh: 孟子曰：「矯枉過正。」

translations.literal: Mencius states: 'Straightening the crooked exceeds the straight.'
translations.idiomatic: Mencius: correcting excess can overshoot the mean.

sentences.zh: 校勘記

translations.literal: Collation notes
translations.idiomatic: Textual collation notes

sentences.zh: 一八一五頁三行張晧按：集解引惠棟說，謂蜀志「晧」作「浩」。

translations.literal: Page 1815 line 3: Zhang Hao. Collation: The Jijie cites Hui Dong, who says the Shu zhi writes the name as Hao.
translations.idiomatic: Editorial: Shu zhi variant 浩 for 晧.

sentences.zh: 一八一五頁四行*

translations.literal: Page 1815 line 4: manuscript asterisk marking editorial lacuna.
translations.idiomatic: Collation note for page 1815, line 4: marginal asterisk in the manuscript.

translations.literal: (chu)
translations.idiomatic: (chu)

sentences.zh: *永元中歸仕州郡據刊誤刪。

translations.literal: *In Yongyuan returned to serve commanderies' — according to Kanwu deleted.
translations.idiomatic: Kanwu deletes the Yongyuan clause as spurious.

sentences.zh: 一八一六頁一五行司徒辟高第為*[侍]*御史腢書治要「御」上有「侍」字，又御覽七七八引及初學記一二引續漢書，並作「侍御史」，今據補。

translations.literal: Page 1816 line 15: summoned with high rank as *[attending]* imperial clerk — the Qunshu zhiyao has shi above yu; Imperial Digest 778 and Chuxue ji 12 citing Xu Han shu all read attending imperial clerk; now supplied per that evidence.
translations.idiomatic: Editorial adds shi (attending) before imperial clerk.

sentences.zh: 一八一七頁一二行多樹諂諛按：「諂」原斗「滔」，逕改正。

translations.literal: Page 1817 line 12: 'raised many flatterers' — collation: 'chan' was miswritten 'tao'; emended per received texts.
translations.idiomatic: Chan corrected from tao.

sentences.zh: 一八一七頁一五行天下號曰八俊按：集解引惠棟說，謂「八俊」續漢書作「八彥」。

translations.literal: Page 1817 line 15: the empire called them Eight Exemplars — collation: Hui Dong says Xu Han shu reads Eight Yan instead of Eight Jun.
translations.idiomatic: Variant Eight Yan for Eight Jun.

sentences.zh: 一八一八頁一三行身絕血嗣按：集解引惠棟說，謂據注則正文注文之「嗣」字皆當作「祀」。

translations.literal: Page 1818 line 13: 'cut off blood heirs' — collation: Hui Dong says according to the commentary both text and gloss should read si (sacrifice) not si (heir).
translations.idiomatic: Si should be 'sacrifice' not 'heir' per commentary.

sentences.zh: 一八一八頁一五行乃嬰等更生之*

translations.literal: Page 1818 line 15: 'then for Ying and others the dawn of*'
translations.idiomatic: Collation on Zhang Gang's speech to Zhang Ying.

translations.literal: (chen)
translations.idiomatic: (chen)

sentences.zh: **[辰]*也按：校補謂「晨」當作「辰」，各本均未正。

translations.literal: **[chen]* also — collation supplement says chen should be chen (the graph 辰); all editions missed the correction.
translations.idiomatic: Chen emended from morning graph per supplement.

sentences.zh: 今據改。

translations.literal: Now emended accordingly.
translations.idiomatic: Emended.

sentences.zh: 一八一九頁九行張嬰等五百餘人按：校補引柳從辰說，謂袁紀作「三百餘人」。

translations.literal: Page 1819 line 9: 'Zhang Ying and more than five hundred' — collation supplement cites Liu Congchen: Yuan ji reads 'more than three hundred.'
translations.idiomatic: Yuan ji says three hundred, not five hundred.

sentences.zh: 一八二一頁一二行稱為*

translations.literal: Page 1821 line 12: titled*
translations.idiomatic: Collation on the compound titled refined promoters.

translations.literal: (ya)
translations.idiomatic: (ya)

sentences.zh: **[推]*士據汲本、殿本改。

translations.literal: **[tui]* scholars — according to Ji and Palace editions emended.
translations.idiomatic: Tui restored per Ji and Palace editions.

sentences.zh: 一八二二頁六行夫挾太山以超*[北]*海據汲本、殿本補。

translations.literal: Page 1822 line 6: 'to clasp Mount Tai and leap over the *[north]* sea' — supplied per Ji and Palace editions.
translations.idiomatic: Bei inserted in Tai/bei idiom per editions.

sentences.zh: 一八二五頁三行陰行善化按：「化」字疑衍。

translations.literal: Page 1825 line 3: 'secretly practiced good transformation' — collation: hua may be redundant.
translations.idiomatic: Suspect redundant hua.

sentences.zh: 史記作「陰行善」，無「化」字。

translations.literal: The Records reads 'secretly practiced good' without hua.
translations.idiomatic: Shiji lacks hua.

sentences.zh: 殿本「化」作「行」，疑涉上「行」字而衍。

translations.literal: Palace edition writes hua as xing — suspect contamination from preceding xing.
translations.idiomatic: Palace variant suggests scribal error.

sentences.zh: 一八二六頁一二行*

translations.literal: Page 1826 line 12: manuscript asterisk in the received text.
translations.idiomatic: Collation for page 1826, line 12 marks a break in the compound.

translations.literal: (jin)
translations.idiomatic: (jin)

sentences.zh: **[乃]*洛陽吏邪據汲本改。

translations.literal: **[nai]* a Luoyang clerk — emended per Ji edition.
translations.idiomatic: Nai emended per Ji edition.

sentences.zh: 一八二七頁八行馳命奏之刊誤謂案文多一「命」字。

translations.literal: Page 1827 line 8: 'galloped off orders and memorialized' — Kanwu says the text has one extra ming.
translations.idiomatic: Kanwu deletes duplicate ming.

sentences.zh: 按：通鑒作「馳還奏之」。

translations.literal: Collation: Zizhi tongjian reads 'galloped back and memorialized.'
translations.idiomatic: Tongjian reads 'galloped back.'

sentences.zh: 一八二七頁一一行自前刺史朱輔按：集解引惠棟說，謂西南夷傳作「酺」，東觀記有傳，仍作「輔」。

translations.literal: Page 1827 line 11: 'former governor Zhu Fu' — collation: Hui Dong says Southwestern Yi biography writes Zhu Pu; Dongguan ji biography still reads Fu.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Fu vs Zhu Pu variant.

sentences.zh: 一八二七頁一二行而二府畏懦按：御覽六四一引謝承書「二」作「三」。

translations.literal: Page 1827 line 12: 'but the two offices were timid' — Imperial Digest 641 citing Xie Cheng's book reads three instead of two.
translations.idiomatic: Two vs three offices in Yulan.

sentences.zh: 一八二七頁一三行聚黨數百人按：汲本、殿本作「百餘人」。

translations.literal: Page 1827 line 13: 'gathered a faction of several hundred' — Ji and Palace editions read 'more than a hundred.'
translations.idiomatic: Hundred-plus vs several hundred.

sentences.zh: 一八三０頁一二行左中郎劉范諫議大夫馬宇按：集解引錢大昕說，謂董卓傳云「侍中馬宇、右中郎將劉范」。

translations.literal: Page 1830 line 12: Palace Gentleman of the Left Liu Fan and Remonstrance Counselor Ma Yu — collation: Qian Daxin says Dong Zhuo zhuan has Attendant Ma Yu and General of the Right Liu Fan.
translations.idiomatic: Titles differ in Dong Zhuo zhuan.

sentences.zh: 一八三０頁一四行在長安西十五里也按：集解引惠棟說，謂紀注及董卓傳注皆雲去長安五十里。

translations.literal: Page 1830 line 14: 'fifteen li west of Chang'an' — collation: Hui Dong says annals commentary and Dong Zhuo zhuan commentary all say fifty li.
translations.idiomatic: Fifteen vs fifty li distance.

sentences.zh: 一八三一頁二行太守怒而撾督郵按：「撾」原作「檛」，逕據汲本、殿本改。

translations.literal: Page 1831 line 2: 'the governor angrily beat the director of retainers' — zhua was originally written with the tree radical; emended per Ji and Palace.
translations.idiomatic: The collation note records that the graph for 'beat' was originally written with the wood radical and was corrected to zhua in the Ji and Palace editions; the commentary uses the same form.

sentences.zh: 注同。

translations.literal: Commentary likewise.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary emended the same way.

sentences.zh: 一八三一頁一０行而州兵朱蓋等反集解引汪文台說，謂御覽二百六十、三百四十七、類聚六十引張璠漢記作「朱益」。

translations.literal: Page 1831 line 10: 'but provincial troops Zhu Gai and others rebelled' — collation: Wang Wentai says Yulan 260, 347 and Leiju 60 citing Zhang Fan Han ji read Zhu Yi.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Gai vs Zhu Yi in sources.

sentences.zh: 今按：影印宋本御覽三四七作「朱蓋」。

translations.literal: Now note: Song woodblock Yulan 347 reads Zhu Gai.
translations.idiomatic: Song Yulan keeps Zhu Gai.

sentences.zh: 一八三一頁一二行受任一邦按：集解引惠棟說，謂球，漢人，不應斥高祖諱。

translations.literal: Page 1831 line 12: 'receive charge of one region' — collation: Hui Dong says Qiu was a Han man and should not use the High Founder's taboo graph for state; Zhang Fan Han ji writes commandery for region.
translations.idiomatic: Bang taboo: Zhang Fan uses jun.

sentences.zh: 張璠漢記「邦」作「郡」。

translations.literal: Zhang Fan's Han ji writes 'commandery' for the graph bang (state/region).
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Fan's Han ji substitutes jun for bang to avoid the taboo on Gaozu's name.

sentences.zh: 一八三二頁六行*

translations.literal: Page 1832 line 6: manuscript asterisk before the graph for fu.
translations.idiomatic: Collation for page 1832, line 6 marks the emendation from fu to summoned.

translations.literal: (fu)
translations.idiomatic: (fu)

sentences.zh: **[征]*拜廷尉刊誤謂案球初未嘗為廷尉，何得言「復」，當作「征」字。

translations.literal: **[summoned]* as minister of justice — Kanwu says Qiu had never been minister of justice before, so fu (again) is wrong; should be zheng (summoned).
translations.idiomatic: Fu emended to summoned; Kanwu notes Chen Qiu was not previously minister of justice.

sentences.zh: 集解引汪文台說，謂書鈔五十五引謝承書，云「橋玄表球明法律，征拜廷尉正」。

translations.literal: Collation: Wang Wentai says Shuchao 55 citing Xie Cheng's book has Qiao Xuan memorializing that Qiu understood the law and was summoned minister of justice director.
translations.idiomatic: Xie Cheng variant: summoned as zheng (director under minister of justice).

sentences.zh: 今據改。

translations.literal: Now emended accordingly.
translations.idiomatic: Emended per collation.

sentences.zh: 一八三三頁三行然*[後]*大言曰據汲本、殿本補。

translations.literal: Page 1833 line 3: then*[after]* he spoke loudly — supplied per Ji and Palace.
translations.idiomatic: Hou inserted per editions.

sentences.zh: 一八三三頁九行鹹字符貞按：集解引惠棟說，謂蔡邕太尉李公碑雲鹹字符卓，案靈紀及胡廣傳注，皆雲字符卓也。

translations.literal: Page 1833 line 9: Xian styled Zhenzhen — collation: Hui Dong says Cai Yong's stele for Grand Commandant Li reads Xian styled Zhuo; Ling annals and Hu Guang zhuan commentary all say styled Zhuo.
translations.idiomatic: Li Xian's style: Zhenzhen vs Zhuo per stele.

sentences.zh: 一八三四頁五行合兄侍中儵按：殿本「儵」作「鯈」。

translations.literal: Page 1834 line 5: He's elder brother Palace Attendant You — Palace edition writes You with fish radical.
translations.idiomatic: Graph variant for Liu You's name.

sentences.zh: 一八三五頁二行球*

translations.literal: Page 1835 line 2: Qiu*
translations.idiomatic: Collation on Chen Qiu's kin.

translations.literal: (elder brother)
translations.idiomatic: (elder brother)

sentences.zh: **[弟]*子珪據殿本改，與正文合。

translations.literal: **[younger brother]* son Gui — emended per Palace edition to match main text.
translations.idiomatic: Xiong/di emended: nephew Gui aligns with text.

sentences.zh: 一八三五頁五行陳元龍淮海之士校補引柳從辰說，謂魏志「淮海」作「湖海」，御覽七百六引同。

translations.literal: Page 1835 line 5: 'Chen Yuanlong man between Huai and sea' — collation supplement cites Liu Congchen: Wei zhi reads lakes and seas instead of Huai and sea; Yulan 706 agrees.
translations.idiomatic: Huaihai vs huhai variant.

sentences.zh: 按：影宋本御覽作「河海」。

translations.literal: Now note: Song photoreprint Yulan reads rivers and seas.
translations.idiomatic: Song Yulan reads hehai.
